content: The third edition of the Exercise Technique Manual for Resistance Training was published by the National Strength and Conditioning Association. The Library of Congress Cataloging-in-Publication Data for the book is GV546.E96 2016 613.7'1--dc23 2015016764. The ISBN for the print version of the book is 978-1-4925-0692-8. Copyright for the book is held by the National Strength and Conditioning Association and the NSCA Certification Commission. Copyright for the book was first established in 1997. The web addresses cited in the text were current as of October 2015, unless otherwise noted.
summary: Basic information about the Exercise Technique Manual for Resistance Training, including its publisher, ISBN, copyright details, and publication history.

content: The book was edited by Matthew Sandstead, Scott Caulfield, and Douglas Berninger. The National Strength and Conditioning Association (NSCA) developed this manual primarily for those preparing for the Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialist (CSCS), NSCA-Certified Personal Trainer (NSCA-CPT), Certified Special Population Specialist (CSPS), and Tactical Strength and Conditioning Facilitator (TSAC-F) certification exams. When used in conjunction with the online video clips, exam candidates will become especially prepared for questions relating to anatomy, biomechanics, program design (e.g., exercise selection), and exercise technique.
summary: Information about the book's editors and its intended audience, particularly those preparing for NSCA certifications.

content: Exercise Technique Manual for Resistance Training, Third Edition, also serves as an excellent resource for strength and conditioning professionals, health and fitness instructors, and personal trainers using resistance training exercises in their own programs or when instructing others. College and university faculty and students will find that this manual and the online video clips complement hands-on instruction and aid in teaching exercise technique without requiring the use of a weight room.
summary: Additional uses for the manual, including its value for professionals, instructors, and students.

content: This manual describes proper technique for 54 free weight and 16 machine exercises. The exercise technique checklists identify the primary muscle groups involved and the correct grip, stance, body position, and range of motion for each exercise. Additionally, descriptions of joint actions, spotting suggestions, and tips for avoiding injury are provided.
summary: Description of the manual's content, including the number of exercises covered and the details provided for each.

content: Although the exercise technique checklists were written by experts, no one should attempt to perform a new exercise without the supervision of a certified professional. It is also recommended that anyone considering participating in an exercise program consult a physician before beginning the program.
summary: Important safety precautions for using the manual and exercising.

content: New to this edition is high-definition online streaming video. The video clips demonstrate correct and incorrect technique for every exercise in the book. The online video can be accessed by visiting www.HumanKinetics.com/ExerciseTechniqueManualForResistanceTraining.
summary: Information about the new addition of online video content and how to access it.

content: The exercises are categorized into the following groups: Power and explosive (total body), Hip and thigh (multijoint and single joint), Calf (single joint), Chest (multijoint and single joint), Back (multijoint), Shoulder (multijoint and single joint), Biceps (single joint), Triceps (single joint), Forearm (single joint), Core, Alternative modes and nontraditional implements. All exercises are illustrated in the online video, which can be accessed at www.HumanKinetics.com/ExerciseTechniqueManualForResistanceTraining.
summary: Categorization of exercises and access to online illustrations.

content: Multijoint exercises involve two or more joints that change angles during the execution of a repetition. Single-joint exercises allow movement in only one joint during a repetition. For the purposes of this manual, the joints of the shoulder girdle are combined with the true (glenohumeral) shoulder joint and, therefore, treated as one joint.
summary: Definitions of multijoint and single-joint exercises and how the shoulder girdle is treated in this manual.

content: For example, the lateral shoulder raise exercise is classified as a single-joint movement, despite the obvious involvement of several shoulder girdle joints in addition to the glenohumeral joint. Additionally, only the predominant muscles are included. Although many other muscles may assist during the exercise or may function as stabilizers, they are not included in the muscular involvement charts.
summary: Further explanation of single-joint classification and the focus on predominant muscles.

content: Each exercise description in this manual includes details about the following: Type of exercise, Description of the action of movement, Muscle group or body area trained, Predominant muscle groups and muscles involved, Guidelines for proper exercise technique listed in the order that they are performed.
summary: Components included in each exercise description.

content: Whether the exercise requires a spotter, as designated by the symbol shown here. Exercises that involve movement over the head or over the face need to be spotted to protect the lifter in case the bar or dumbbell falls on the lifter’s head, neck, face, or torso and to help unrack and rack the bar in its supports, when needed.
summary: Information about when a spotter is required and the reasons for spotting.

content: A spotter is also needed when a bar is placed on the back of a lifter’s neck, across the shoulders, or on top of the anterior deltoids or clavicles. To promote safety, overhead exercises and those with the bar on the back or front of the shoulders should be performed inside a power rack with the crossbars set at an appropriate height based on the type of exercise and height of the lifter.
summary: Specific exercises that require a spotter and safety precautions for spotting.

content: The spotter (or spotters) needs to be as tall as the lifter and strong enough to be able to support the load, if necessary. Out-of-the-rack exercises (e.g., forward step lunge or step-up) with heavy weights can result in serious injury and should be spotted.
summary: Requirements for a spotter and exercises that should be spotted.

content: Exercises in these situations include the following: Any variation of the shoulder press, Any variation of the barbell squat, Any variation of the barbell lunge. The spotter should grasp the bar with an alternated grip, usually narrower than the lifter’s grip, when preparing for over-the-face exercises. The curved trajectory of the bar in some exercises (e.g., lying triceps extension) causes the spotter to have to use an alternated grip to pick up the bar and return it to the floor; however, a supinated grip should be used to spot the bar during execution of the exercise.
summary: Specific exercises that require spotting and proper grip techniques for the spotter.

content: Doing so helps ensure that the bar does not roll out of the spotter’s hands and onto the lifter’s face or neck. The spotter needs to establish a solid, wide base of support with the feet and maintain a neutral spine position to support the load that may have to be lifted in assistance of the lifter.
summary: Additional safety tips for the spotter.

content: Exercises in these situations include the following: Any variation of the shoulder press, Any variation of the barbell squat, Any variation of the barbell lunge.
summary: This chunk lists exercises that require a spotter.

content: The spotter should grasp the bar with an alternated grip, usually narrower than the lifter’s grip, when preparing for over-the-face exercises. The curved trajectory of the bar in some exercises (e.g., lying triceps extension) causes the spotter to have to use an alternated grip to pick up the bar and return it to the floor; however, a supinated grip should be used to spot the bar during execution of the exercise. Doing so helps ensure that the bar does not roll out of the spotter’s hands and onto the lifter’s face or neck.
summary: This chunk describes proper spotter technique for exercises where the bar moves overhead.

content: The spotter needs to establish a solid, wide base of support with the feet and maintain a neutral spine position to support the load that may have to be lifted in assistance of the lifter.
summary: This chunk outlines the spotter's stance and posture.

content: Exercises in these situations include the following: Any variation of the bench press, Lying barbell triceps extension, Any variation of the dumbbell fly.
summary: This chunk lists exercises that require a spotter.

content: Follow these guidelines to ensure safe exercise technique: Perform power and explosive exercises in a clean, dry, flat, well-marked area (e.g., on a lifting platform) free of obstacles and people. This guideline can also apply to other complex nonpower exercises such as the lunge, deadlift, and step-up.
summary: This chunk provides safety guidelines for power and explosive exercises.

content: If a repetition in a power or explosive exercise cannot be completed, push forward on the bar to move the body backward and let the bar fall to the floor. Do not attempt to save a missed or failed repetition of this type of exercise.
summary: This chunk describes how to handle a failed repetition in a power or explosive exercise.

content: Before performing exercises that finish with the bar overhead, check to see if there is sufficient floor-to-ceiling space. Use a bar with revolving sleeves, especially for the power and explosive exercises.
summary: This chunk provides safety guidelines for exercises that finish overhead.

content: Use a squat or power rack with the supporting pins or hooks set to position the bar at armpit height for the front squat and back squat; using that setting when beginning or ending an exercise with the bar at shoulder height is preferred to beginning or ending with the bar on the floor. When lifting the bar up and out of the supporting pins or hooks of a squat or power rack in preparation for an exercise, always step backward at the beginning of the set and step forward at the end of the set. Do not walk backward to return the bar to the rack.
summary: This chunk describes proper rack usage for squats and other exercises.

content: Always use collars and locks to secure free weight plates on the bar. Fully insert the selectorized pin or key (usually L or T shaped) into the weight stack for machine exercises.
summary: This chunk emphasizes safety precautions for weightlifting.

content: The incline dumbbell bench press or fly, upright row, and lying barbell triceps extension are exercises that should be performed with proper body positioning to avoid excessive strain on the low back. To avoid excessive strain on the low back, place the body in the correct position to lift the weight safely and effectively.
summary: This chunk highlights proper form for exercises that can strain the lower back.

content: When squatting behind a bar, place the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart. If lifting a bar, position the bar close to the shins and over the balls of the feet and grasp the bar with a closed grip that is shoulder-width (or slightly wider) apart. If lifting dumbbells, stand directly between them and grasp the handles with a closed grip and a neutral arm or hand position.
summary: This chunk provides instructions for proper squat and dumbbell lift setup.

content: Position the arms outside the knees with the elbows extended. The back should be neutral or slightly arched when lifting a weight. The trapezius should be relaxed and slightly stretched, the chest held up and out, and the shoulder blades held together when lifting a weight.
summary: This chunk describes proper upper body positioning during lifts.

content: The head should be in line with the spine or slightly hyperextended when lifting a weight. The body’s weight should be balanced between the middle and balls of the feet, but the heels should be in contact with the floor when lifting a weight. The shoulders should be over or slightly in front of the bar when lifting a weight. The eyes should be focused straight ahead or slightly upward when lifting a weight.
summary: This chunk describes proper head, foot, and shoulder positioning during lifts.

content: A weight belt may help maintain intra-abdominal pressure while lifting. It is recommended that a weight belt be worn for exercises that place stress on the low back and during sets that use near-maximal or maximal loads. A weight belt should not be worn for exercises that do not stress the low back (e.g., biceps curl, lat pulldown) or for exercises that do stress the low back but involve the use of light loads (e.g., back squat, deadlift).
summary: This chunk outlines when to use a weight belt for lifting.

content: The best general guideline about proper breathing during a resistance training exercise is to exhale through the sticking point (the most difficult part of the exercise) of the concentric (exertion) phase and inhale during the easier part of the exercise (eccentric phase). The sticking point of the free weight bench press exercise occurs about halfway through the upward movement phase. At the sticking point of the free weight bench press exercise, the lifter should exhale through this portion of the movement. As the bar is lowered back down to the chest, the lifter should inhale.
summary: This chunk describes proper breathing technique during weightlifting.

content: For experienced and well-trained lifters performing structural exercises (those that load the vertebral column and therefore place stress on it) with high loads, the Valsalva maneuver can be helpful for maintaining proper vertebral alignment and support by increasing intra-abdominal pressure. The Valsalva maneuver involves expiring against a closed glottis, which, when combined with contracting the abdomen and rib cage muscles, creates rigid compartments of fluid in the lower torso and air in the upper torso (i.e., the fluid ball) and increases pressure within the abdomen. The advantage of the Valsalva maneuver is that it increases the rigidity of the entire torso to aid in supporting the vertebral column, which in turn reduces the associated compressive forces on the disks during lifting. The Valsalva maneuver also helps to establish and maintain a normal lordotic lumbar spine position (also called a neutral spine) and erect upper torso position described in the technique checklists for certain exercises. The resulting increase in intra-abdominal pressure from the Valsalva maneuver has potentially detrimental side effects, such as dizziness, disorientation, excessively high blood pressure, and blackouts. The breath-holding phase of the Valsalva maneuver should be transient, only about 1 to 2 seconds (at most). Even a well-trained lifter should not extend the length of the breath-holding phase, because blood pressure can quickly rise to triple resting levels.
summary: This chunk describes the Valsalva maneuver, its benefits, risks, and proper execution.

content: The Valsalva maneuver increases the rigidity of the entire torso to aid in supporting the vertebral column, which in turn reduces the associated compressive forces on the disks during lifting. It also helps to establish and maintain a normal lordotic lumbar spine position (also called a neutral spine) and erect upper torso position described in the technique checklists for certain exercises.  Even a well-trained lifter should not extend the length of the breath-holding phase, because blood pressure can quickly rise to triple resting levels. Strength and conditioning professionals conducting 1-repetition maximum (1RM) tests in exercises like the power clean, power snatch, squat, deadlift, or bench press should be aware of the advantages and disadvantages of coaching lifters in the Valsalva maneuver. It is important that the vertebral column be internally supported during these movements for safety and technique reasons. It is recommended that a lifter not extend the breath-holding period during the Valsalva maneuver.
summary: The Valsalva maneuver provides spinal support during lifts, but should be performed cautiously due to potential side effects.

content: The resulting increase in intra-abdominal pressure from the Valsalva maneuver has potentially detrimental side effects, such as dizziness, disorientation, excessively high blood pressure, and blackouts. The breath-holding phase of the Valsalva maneuver should be transient, only about 1 to 2 seconds (at most).
summary: The Valsalva maneuver can have negative side effects if the breath-holding phase is too long.

content: Exercises involving alternative modes and nontraditional implements have become more common. The general guidelines for exercises using alternative modes and nontraditional implements are similar to those used with traditional resistance training methods. Guidelines for performing alternative or nontraditional implement exercises include maintaining a stable body position, utilizing an appropriate grip, and following proper breathing patterns.
summary: Alternative training methods share similarities with traditional training, emphasizing proper form and breathing.

content: Bodyweight exercises involve the use of the body’s own weight as a form of external resistance through various movements. Core stability and balance training methods include isolation exercises, the intentional use of machines or free weights, and instability devices to activate the core and improve balance.
summary: Bodyweight exercises and core stability training utilize various methods to challenge balance and core strength.

content: Variable resistance allows the applied resistance to be varied in conjunction with changes in joint angle to maximize muscular force application throughout the full range of motion. Nontraditional implements incorporate strongman exercises and kettlebell training to add greater variation to lifters’ overall training.
summary: Variable resistance and nontraditional implements enhance training variety and muscle activation.

content: Unilateral training is integrated into a training program to reduce bilateral asymmetries or as a rehabilitation tool.
summary: Unilateral training addresses muscle imbalances and aids in rehabilitation.

content: The power snatch involves lifting the bar overhead with the arms fully extended in one continuous movement. The upward movement of the power snatch consists of four distinct phases.
summary: The power snatch is a complex lift with distinct phases.

content: To perform the power snatch, stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the bar evenly with a pronated grip. The hand placement on the bar for the power snatch is wider than it is for other exercises. The lifter’s grip width for the power snatch can be estimated by measuring the distance from the knuckles’ edge of a clenched fist of an arm extended out to the side and parallel to the floor, across the back of the arm and upper back, to the outside edge of the opposite shoulder. Alternatively, the lifter’s grip width can be estimated by measuring the elbow-to-elbow distance when the upper arms are abducted directly out from the sides and parallel to the floor.
summary: The starting position for the power snatch involves specific foot placement, grip width, and body positioning.

content: The actual grip for the power snatch can be a closed grip or a hook grip. To use a hook grip, place a pronated hand on the bar and first wrap the thumb around the bar, then wrap the four fingers. The first one or two fingers, depending on their length, will cover the thumb when using a hook grip. The hook grip is effective for lifting maximal or near-maximal loads, but it can be uncomfortable initially. Wrapping the thumbs with athletic tape will alleviate the pressure when using the hook grip.
summary: The power snatch grip can be closed or hook grip, with the hook grip offering advantages for heavy lifts but requiring adaptation.

content: Position the arms outside the knees with the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides. Position the bar approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. Just before liftoff, observe the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to place the body in the correct position to lift the bar off the floor. All repetitions of the power snatch begin from this position.
summary: The power snatch setup involves precise arm and bar positioning.

content: The exact positions of the torso, hips, knees, and bar in the power snatch are dependent on a lifter’s joint segment lengths and lower body joint flexibility. An inflexible person attempting to get into the correct starting position of the power snatch may have difficulty grasping the bar with the elbows extended while keeping the heels on the floor. If the preparatory body position cannot be achieved, the hang power snatch is an alternative because it does not require the lifter to start with the bar on the floor.
summary: Power snatch starting position is individualized and may require modifications for flexibility limitations.

content: The portion of the upward movement phase from liftoff to where the bar is just above the knees in the power snatch is termed the first pull. Begin the first pull by forcefully extending the hips and knees. The hips and knees must extend at the same rate during the first pull to keep the torso angle constant in relation to the floor. Maintaining a neutral (or slightly arched) spine position while shifting balance slowly from over the middle of the feet toward the heels helps in maintaining a constant torso angle.
summary: The first pull of the power snatch involves synchronized hip and knee extension with a neutral spine.

content: The hang power snatch begins with the bar above the knees. The hang power snatch is similar to the power snatch with the bar starting on the thighs, just above the knees. The hang power snatch requires more muscular effort than the power snatch because there is no initial momentum of the bar at the knees. Stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the bar evenly with a pronated grip. The hand placement on the bar for the hang power snatch is wider than it is for other exercises. The grip width can be estimated by measuring the distance from the knuckles’ edge of a clenched fist of an arm extended out to the side and parallel to the floor, across the back of the arm and upper back, to the outside edge of the opposite shoulder. Alternatively, the lifter’s grip width can be estimated by measuring the elbow-to-elbow distance when the upper arms are abducted directly out from the sides and parallel to the floor.
summary: Description of the hang power snatch, its starting position, grip width, and similarities/differences to the power snatch.

content: The portion of the upward movement phase from liftoff to where the bar is just above the knees in the power snatch is termed the first pull. Begin the first pull by forcefully extending the hips and knees. The hips and knees must extend at the same rate during the first pull to keep the torso angle constant in relation to the floor. Maintaining a neutral (or slightly arched) spine position while shifting balance slowly from over the middle of the feet toward the heels helps in maintaining a constant torso angle. The elbows should be fully extended, the head neutral in relation to the spine, and the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. As the bar is raised, it should be kept as close to the shins as possible; slightly shifting the body’s weight back toward the heels as the bar is lifted will promote proper bar trajectory.
summary: Details on the first pull phase of the power snatch, emphasizing proper technique and body positioning.

content: The upward movement phase where the knees and thighs move forward under the bar is called the transition. As the bar rises to just above the knees, thrust the hips forward and slightly flex the knees to move the thighs against, and the knees under, the bar. During this second knee bend, the body’s weight shifts forward toward the middle of the feet, but the heels remain in contact with the floor. Keep the back neutral or slightly arched, the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides, and the head in line with the spine. The shoulders should still be over the bar, although they will tend to move backward as the knees and thighs move under the bar. The body is in the power position at the end of this phase.
summary: Description of the transition phase in the power snatch, highlighting key movements and body positioning.

content: The upward movement from the power position with the bar at the thighs and close to the body to where the lower body joints are fully extended and the bar has reached its maximum velocity is referred to as the second pull or power phase. The bar should be near to or in contact with the front of the thighs near the inguinal fold. Initiate a fast upward motion by quickly extending the hips, knees, and ankles. Note that ankle extension here refers to plantar flexion. The bar should pass as close to the body as possible. Maintain a torso position with the back neutral or slightly arched, the elbows pointing out to the sides, and the head in line with the spine. Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible while the hips, knees, and ankles are extending. As the lower body joints fully extend, rapidly shrug the shoulders. The elbows should be extended and pointed out to the sides during the shrugging movement.
summary: Explanation of the second pull phase, emphasizing speed, bar trajectory, and upper body engagement.

content: As the shoulders reach their highest elevation, flex the elbows to begin pulling the body under the bar. The upper body movements are similar to the upright row exercise, only with a wider grip. The elbows move up and out to the sides. Continue to pull with the arms as high and as long as possible. Because of the triple extension of the lower body and the pulling effort of the upper body, the torso will be erect or slightly hyperextended, the head will be tilted slightly back, and the feet may lose contact with the floor. The act of receiving the bar in the overhead position is called the catch.
summary: Description of the final stages of the power snatch, including the pull under the bar and the catch.

content: After the lower body has fully extended and the bar reaches near-maximal height, pull the body under the bar by rotating the arms and hands around and then under the bar and by flexing the hips and knees to approximately a quarter-squat position. The feet typically regain contact with the floor in a slightly wider stance with the toes pointed out slightly farther than at the starting position. Once the arms are under the bar, extend the elbows quickly to push the bar upward and the body downward under the bar. The bar should be caught over and slightly behind the ears with fully extended elbows, an erect and stable torso, a neutral head position, flat feet, and the body’s weight over the middle of the feet. The quarter-squat position should be reached with the elbows fully extended just as the bar reaches its maximum height. After gaining control and balance, stand up by extending the hips and knees to a fully erect position.
summary: Detailed steps for the catch and recovery phase of the power snatch, emphasizing proper form and balance.

content: If bumper plates are used, the bar can be returned to the floor with a controlled drop; the bounce of the plates should be controlled with the hands on or near the bar. Most commonly, the bar is lowered slowly from the overhead position by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the shoulders to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the thighs. The hips and knees are simultaneously flexed to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs. The bar is then lowered by squatting down until it touches the floor. Reposition the bar and the body for the next repetition, if applicable.
summary: Instructions for returning the bar to the starting position after a repetition, including options for controlled drops and slow descents.

content: Stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. Position the arms outside the knees with the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides. Lift the bar off the floor and stand erect, so that the bar is near the inguinal fold of the hips. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for the hang power snatch.

content: The hand placement on the bar for the hang power snatch is wider than it is for other exercises. The grip width can be estimated by measuring the distance from the knuckles’ edge of a clenched fist of an arm extended out to the side and parallel to the floor, across the back of the arm and upper back, to the outside edge of the opposite shoulder. Alternatively, the lifter’s grip width can be estimated by measuring the elbow-to-elbow distance when the upper arms are abducted directly out from the sides and parallel to the floor.
summary: Explains how to determine the appropriate grip width for the hang power snatch.

content: The actual grip can be a closed grip or a hook grip. To use a hook grip, place a pronated hand on the bar and first wrap the thumb around the bar, then wrap the four fingers. The first one or two fingers, depending on their length, will cover the thumb. The hook grip is effective for lifting maximal or near-maximal loads, but it can be uncomfortable initially. Wrapping the thumbs with athletic tape will alleviate the pressure when using the hook grip.
summary: Details the different grip options for the hang power snatch and their advantages.

content: The downward movement is initiated by a descent (eccentric contraction of the hamstrings), where the lifter lowers the bar to the midthigh, top of the knee, or below the knee. Balance is shifted from the middle of the foot to closer to the heel as the bar is lowered.
summary: Describes the eccentric phase of the hang power snatch.

content: Once the bar reaches the lowest position of the downward movement, the lifter quickly reverses the action by utilizing the stretch-shortening cycle of the hamstrings to begin to accelerate the bar upward. The hips move forward as the knees slightly flex to move the thighs against, and the knees under, the bar.
summary: Explains the transition phase of the hang power snatch.

content: As the knees flex, the body’s weight shifts forward toward the middle of the feet, but the heels remain in contact with the floor. Keep the back neutral (or slightly arched), the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides, and the head in line with the spine. The shoulders should still be over the bar, although they will tend to move backward as the knees and thighs move under the bar. The body is in the power position at the end of this phase.
summary: Details the body positioning during the power phase of the hang power snatch.

content: The upward movement from the power position with the bar at the thighs and close to the body to where the lower body joints are fully extended and the bar has reached its maximum velocity is referred to as the power phase. The bar should be near to or in contact with the front of the thighs, close to the inguinal fold. Initiate a fast, upward motion by quickly extending the hips, knees, and ankles. Ankle extension here refers to plantar flexion. The bar should pass as close to the body as possible.
summary: Describes the power phase of the hang power snatch.

content: Maintain a torso position with the back neutral or slightly arched, the elbows pointed out to the sides, and the head in line with the spine. Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible while the hips, knees, and ankles are extending. As the lower body joints fully extend, rapidly shrug the shoulders. The elbows should be extended and pointed out to the sides during the shrugging movement.
summary: Details the upper body movements during the power phase of the hang power snatch.

content: As the shoulders reach their highest elevation, flex the elbows to begin pulling the body under the bar with the arms. The upper body movements are similar to the upright row exercise, only with a wider grip. The elbows move up and out to the sides, but not backward. Because of the triple extension of the lower body and the pulling effort of the upper body, the torso will be erect or slightly hyperextended, the head will be tilted slightly back, and the feet may lose contact with the floor.
summary: Describes the final phase of the hang power snatch, where the body is pulled under the bar.

content: The act of receiving the bar in the overhead position is called the catch. After the lower body has fully extended and the bar reaches near-maximal height, pull the body under the bar by quickly rotating the elbows around, and then under, the bar. Once the elbows are under the bar, fully extend the elbows quickly to push the bar upward and the body downward under the bar. The hips and knees simultaneously flex into approximately a quarter-squat position. The feet typically regain contact with the floor by quickly moving into a slightly wider stance with the toes pointed out slightly farther than at the starting position.
summary: Explains the catch phase of the hang power snatch.

content: The act of receiving the bar in the overhead position is called the catch. The bar should be caught over and slightly behind the ears with fully extended elbows, an erect and tight torso, a neutral head position, flat feet, and the body’s weight over the middle of the feet. Ideally, the quarter-squat position will be reached with the elbows extended just as the bar reaches its maximum height.  Most commonly, the bar is lowered slowly from the overhead position by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the shoulders to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the thighs. The hips and knees are simultaneously flexed to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs. Reposition the bar and the body for the next repetition, if applicable.
summary: Describes the catch phase and descent of the overhead lift, emphasizing proper form and control.

content: After the lower body has fully extended and the bar reaches near-maximal height, pull the body under the bar by quickly rotating the elbows around, and then under, the bar. Once the elbows are under the bar, fully extend the elbows quickly to push the bar upward and the body downward under the bar. The hips and knees simultaneously flex into approximately a quarter-squat position. The feet typically regain contact with the floor by quickly moving into a slightly wider stance with the toes pointed out slightly farther than at the starting position. After gaining control and balance, stand up by extending the hips and knees to a fully erect position.
summary: Details the upward movement of the overhead lift, focusing on the mechanics of pulling the body under the bar and extending to stand.

content: From the starting position, the muscle snatch exercise involves lifting the bar overhead with the arms fully extended. This is slightly different from the power snatch, which is one continuous movement. The muscle snatch is divided into two movements: The elbows rotate under the bar and then the bar is pressed out to finish the movement. The hips and knees do not flex to receive the bar in the muscle snatch. Stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. The squat grip is wider than in other exercises. The hand placement on the bar can be estimated by measuring the distance from the knuckles’ edge of a clenched fist of an arm extended out to the side and parallel to the floor, across the back of the arm and upper back, to the outside edge of the opposite shoulder.
summary: Explains the muscle snatch technique, differentiating it from the power snatch and outlining the starting position and grip.

content: Straddle a dumbbell on the floor and place the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the dumbbell with a closed, pronated grip with the elbow fully extended. The arm that is not holding the dumbbell should hang down next to that side of the body. Just before liftoff, observe the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to place the body in the correct position to lift the dumbbell off the floor. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by forcefully extending the hips, knees, and ankles (commonly called the triple extension). The dumbbell should slide up the thigh, or remain very close to the thigh, as it accelerates upward. The elbow of the arm holding the dumbbell should remain extended as the knees, hips, and ankles are extending. As the lower body joints fully extend, rapidly shrug the shoulder of the arm holding the dumbbell. The elbow of the arm holding the dumbbell should be extended and pointed out to the side during the shrugging movement. As the shoulder reaches its highest elevation, flex the elbow holding the dumbbell to begin pulling the body under the dumbbell. The dumbbell should pass as close to the torso as possible. Continue to pull with the arm holding the dumbbell as high and as long as possible. The arm that is not holding the dumbbell should remain on the opposite hip or be held to the side. Because of the triple extension of the lower body and the pulling effort of the upper body, the torso will be erect, the head will be tilted slightly back, and the feet may lose contact with the floor. After the lower body has fully extended and the dumbbell reaches near-maximal height, pull the body under the dumbbell by rotating the arm and hand holding the dumbbell around and then under the dumbbell and by flexing the hips and knees to approximately a quarter-squat position. Once the arm holding the dumbbell is under the dumbbell, extend the elbow quickly to push the dumbbell up and the body downward under the dumbbell. The quarter-squat position should be reached with the elbow of the arm holding the dumbbell fully extended just as the dumbbell reaches its maximum height. The arm that is not holding the dumbbell should remain on the opposite hip or be held to the side. After gaining control and balance, stand up to a fully erect position. At the completion of the repetition, slowly lower the dumbbell from the overhead position by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the shoulder of the arm holding the dumbbell; allow a controlled descent of the dumbbell first to the shoulder, then the thigh, and finally to the floor between the feet, using a squatting movement. Reposition the dumbbell and the body for the next repetition, if applicable.
summary: Describes the dumbbell snatch exercise, outlining the starting position, movement phases, and proper form.

content: The muscle snatch is divided into two movements: The elbows rotate under the bar and then the bar is pressed out to finish the movement. The hips and knees do not flex to receive the bar in the muscle snatch.
summary: The muscle snatch involves two movements: elbows rotating under the bar followed by a press, without hip or knee flexion.

content: Stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. The squat grip is wider than in other exercises. The lifter’s grip width can be estimated by measuring the elbow-to-elbow distance when the upper arms are abducted directly out from the sides and parallel to the floor. The hand placement on the bar can be estimated by measuring the distance from the knuckles’ edge of a clenched fist of an arm extended out to the side and parallel to the floor, across the back of the arm and upper back, to the outside edge of the opposite shoulder.
summary: The muscle snatch requires a wider than usual squat grip, with hand and elbow placement determined by specific measurements.

content: The actual grip can be a closed grip or a hook grip. To use a hook grip, place a pronated hand on the bar and first wrap the thumb around the bar, then wrap the four fingers. The first one or two fingers, depending on their length, will cover the thumb. The hook grip is effective for lifting maximal or near-maximal loads, but it can be uncomfortable initially. Wrapping the thumbs with athletic tape will alleviate the pressure when using the hook grip.
summary: The muscle snatch grip can be either closed or hook, with the latter being more suitable for heavy lifts but requiring adaptation.

content: Position the arms outside the knees with the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides. Position the bar approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. All repetitions begin from the preparatory body position. Exact positions of the torso, hips, knees, and bar are dependent on a lifter’s segment lengths and lower body joint flexibility. An inflexible person attempting to get into the correct starting position of the muscle snatch may have difficulty grasping the bar with the elbows extended while keeping the heels on the floor.
summary: The muscle snatch starting position emphasizes extended elbows, bar placement, and individual flexibility considerations.

content: The lifter can perform the exercise from the hang starting position as an alternative because it does not require the lifter to start with the bar on the floor. The hang starting position begins with the bar above the knees.
summary: An alternative starting position for the muscle snatch is the hang, which avoids the need to lift from the floor.

content: The portion of the upward movement phase from liftoff to where the bar is just above the knees is termed the first pull. Begin the exercise by forcefully extending the hips and knees. These joints must extend at the same rate to keep the torso angle constant in relation to the floor. Do not let the hips rise before or faster than the shoulders. Maintaining the neutral (or slightly arched) back position while shifting balance slowly from over the middle of the feet toward the heels helps in maintaining a constant torso angle. The elbows should still be fully extended, the head in line with the spine, and the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar.
summary: The first pull of the muscle snatch involves simultaneous hip and knee extension, maintaining a neutral back, and proper bar trajectory.

content: As the bar is raised, it should be kept as close to the shins as possible; slightly shifting the body’s weight back toward the heels as the bar is lifted will promote proper bar trajectory. The portion of the upward movement where the knees and thighs move forward under the bar is called the transition. As the bar rises to just above the knees, thrust the hips forward and slightly flex the knees to move the thighs against, and the knees under, the bar.
summary: The muscle snatch transition involves shifting weight, moving the knees under the bar, and preparing for the power phase.

content: During this second knee bend, the body’s weight shifts forward toward the middle of the feet, but the heels remain in contact with the floor. Keep the back neutral or slightly arched, the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides, and the head in line with the spine. Keep the shoulders over the bar, although they will tend to move backward as the knees and thighs move under the bar. The body is in the power position at the end of this phase.
summary: The transition phase of the muscle snatch culminates in the power position, characterized by a neutral back, extended elbows, and proper shoulder alignment.

content: The upward movement from the power position with the bar at the thighs and close to the body to where the lower body joints are fully extended and the bar has reached its maximum velocity is referred to as the second pull or power phase. Ankle extension in this context refers to plantar flexion. The bar should pass as close to the body as possible during the movement. Maintain a torso position with a neutral or slightly arched back, elbows pointed out to the sides, and the head in line with the spine. Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible while the hips, knees, and ankles are extending. As the lower body joints fully extend, rapidly shrug the shoulders. The elbows should be extended and pointed out to the sides during the shrugging movement. As the shoulders reach their highest elevation, flex the elbows to begin pulling the bar up. The upper body movements are similar to the upright row exercise, only with a wider grip.
summary: The power phase of the muscle snatch involves explosive extension of the lower body, maintaining a neutral torso, and culminating in a shrug to lift the bar.

content: Stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the bar evenly with a shoulder-width (or slightly wider), pronated grip. The arms should be positioned outside the knees with the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides. The bar should be positioned approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. All repetitions begin from the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines. Exact positions of the torso, hips, knees, and bar are related to the lifter’s body segment length and lower body joint flexibility.
summary: Describes the starting position and general guidelines for the power clean.

content: Begin the first pull by forcefully extending the hips and knees. Keep the torso angle constant in relation to the floor, do not let the hips rise before or faster than the shoulders, and keep the back neutral or slightly arched. Maintain full elbow extension, with the head in line with the spine, and the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. Keep the bar as close to the shins as possible. As the bar rises to just above the knees, thrust the hips forward and slightly flex the knees to move the thighs against, and the knees under, the bar. As the knees flex, shift the body’s weight forward toward the middle of the feet, keeping the heels on the floor. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back, with the elbows fully extended and pointed out to the sides, the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar, and the head in line with the spine.
summary: Details the first pull phase of the power clean.

content: From this position with the bar on the thighs between the knees and middle of the thighs, start the second pull (power phase) by forcefully and quickly extending the hips, knees, and ankles. The bar should pass as close to the torso as possible. Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible while the hips, knees, and ankles are extending. As the lower body joints fully extend, rapidly shrug the shoulders. The elbows should be kept extended and pointed out to the sides during the shrugging movement. As the shoulders reach their highest elevation, flex the elbows to begin pulling the body under the bar. Continue to pull with the arms as high and as long as possible with the elbows moving up and out to the sides. The upward momentum from the triple extension will result in an erect or slightly hyperextended torso and head, and the feet may come off the floor.
summary: Describes the second pull (power phase) and the finishing movement of the power clean.

content: If bumper plates are used, the bar can be returned to the floor with a controlled drop; the bounce of the plates should be controlled with the hands on or near the bar. Most commonly, the bar is lowered slowly from the overhead position by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the upper body to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the thighs. The hips and knees are simultaneously flexed to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs. The bar is then lowered by squatting down until it touches the floor. Reposition the bar and the body for the next repetition, if applicable.
summary: Outlines the process of returning the bar to the floor after a successful power clean.

content: This exercise is similar to the power snatch but with two major differences: the final bar position is at the shoulders, not over the head, and the grip is approximately shoulder-width apart, whereas the snatch has a considerably wider grip.
summary: Compares the power clean to the power snatch, highlighting key differences.

content: An alternative exercise is the hang power clean, which begins with the bar just above the knees instead of on the floor.
summary: Introduces the hang power clean as a variation of the power clean.

content: The hang power clean is a variation of the power clean exercise that starts at the beginning of the transition. The hang power clean involves lifting the bar from a position just above the knees. The hang power clean requires more muscular effort than the power clean because there is no initial momentum of the bar at the knees. The hang power clean involves a forceful, rapid extension of the hips, knees, and ankles followed by the shrugging of the shoulders and pulling with the arms. The starting position for the hang power clean is the same as the power clean: the same stance, grip, and initial body position. The bar should be lifted along the shins and thighs until standing erect with the bar resting on the front of the thighs. From the standing position, lean forward and flex the hips and knees to place the bar just above the knees. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: The hang power clean is a variation of the power clean starting at the knees, requiring more effort due to the lack of initial momentum.

content: The upward movement of the hang power clean starts with a forceful extension of the hips, knees, and ankles (triple extension). Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible during the triple extension. As the lower body joints fully extend, rapidly shrug the shoulders but keep the elbows extended and pointed out to the sides. At maximal shoulder elevation, flex the elbows and pull the body under the bar. The bar should pass as close to the torso as possible during the upward movement. Continue to pull with the arms as high and as long as possible to achieve the highest bar position. The triple extension will result in an erect or slightly hyperextended torso and head, and the feet may come off the floor.
summary: The hang power clean's upward movement involves a triple extension, shoulder shrug, and pulling the body under the bar.

content: After the lower body has fully extended and the bar reaches maximal height, pull the body under the bar by rotating the arms and hands around and then under the bar and by flexing the hips and knees to approximately a quarter-squat position. Catch the bar at the anterior deltoids and clavicles with the elbows slightly forward of the bar. The feet will regain contact with the floor in a slightly wider stance in comparison to the starting position after catching the bar. After gaining control and balance, stand up to a fully erect position. At the completion of the repetition, rotate the arms and hands back around the bar to unrack it from the anterior deltoids and clavicles. Slowly lower the bar down to the thighs, slightly flexing the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs. If additional repetitions are to be performed, stand fully erect first and then follow the guidelines to move the body into the correct starting position. The bar does not return to the floor between repetitions. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: The hang power clean's catch and lowering phases involve controlling the bar's descent and maintaining an erect torso.

content: The dumbbell hang power clean begins with grasping two dumbbells with a closed grip.
summary: The dumbbell hang power clean is a variation using dumbbells.

content: The dumbbell hang power clean begins with grasping two dumbbells with a closed grip. The lifter should observe the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to place the body in the correct position to lift the dumbbells off the floor. The dumbbells should be lifted slowly along the shins and knees until standing erect with the dumbbells resting on the front of the thighs, or near the sides of the thighs. From the standing position with arms fully extended, the lifter should lean forward and flex the hips and knees to place the dumbbells slightly above or below the knees, depending on the lifter’s torso and arms length. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position and initial lift of the dumbbell hang power clean.

content: The upward movement begins with forcefully extending the hips, knees, and ankles (triple extension). The dumbbells should slide up the thighs, or remain very close to the thighs, as they accelerate upward. The shoulders should be kept over the dumbbells and the elbows extended as long as possible. As the lower body joints fully extend, the lifter should rapidly shrug the shoulders but keep the elbows extended and pointed out to the sides. At maximal shoulder elevation, the lifter should flex the elbows. The dumbbells should pass as close to the torso as possible. The lifter should continue to pull with the arms as high and as long as possible. This triple extension will result in an erect or slightly hyperextended torso and head, and the feet may come off the floor.
summary: Details the upward movement of the dumbbell hang power clean, emphasizing triple extension and shoulder elevation.

content: After the lower body has fully extended and the dumbbells reach maximal height, the lifter should pull the body under the dumbbells by rotating the arms and hands around and then under the dumbbells and by flexing the hips and knees to approximately a quarter-squat position. The dumbbells should be caught at the anterior deltoids and clavicles with the elbows slightly forward of the dumbbells. The feet will regain contact with the floor in a slightly wider stance in comparison to the starting position. After gaining control and balance, the lifter should stand up to a fully erect position.
summary: Explains the catching and stabilization phase of the dumbbell hang power clean.

content: At the completion of the repetition, the lifter should rotate the arms and hands back around the dumbbells to unrack them from the anterior deltoids and clavicles, and slowly lower the dumbbells down to the thighs. Slightly flex the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the dumbbells on the thighs. If additional repetitions are to be performed, the lifter should stand fully erect first and then follow the guidelines to move the body into the correct starting position. The dumbbells should not be returned to the floor between repetitions. At the completion of the set, the lifter should slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Outlines the lowering and reset procedures for the dumbbell hang power clean.

content: The push press exercise consists of quickly and forcefully flexing and then extending the hips and knees to push the bar from the shoulders to over the head. The upward movement of the push press consists of two phases but occurs in one continuous motion without interruption. The push press involves a rapid hip and knee extension that accelerates the bar off the shoulders. The hip and knee extension thrust in the push press is only forceful enough to drive the bar one-half to two-thirds the distance overhead. From the height reached by the hip and knee extension thrust, the bar is pressed out to the overhead position with the hips and knees remaining fully extended.
summary: Describes the mechanics of the push press exercise.

content: To begin a push press, use the power clean or hang power clean exercise to lift the bar from the floor to the shoulders or remove the bar from a shoulder-height position on a power or squat rack. Stand erect with the feet hip- to shoulder-width apart and the toes pointed forward or slightly outward for the starting position of the push press. Once the bar is positioned at the front of the shoulders, the grip should be even, pronated, closed, and about shoulder-width apart. The grip should remain relaxed on the bar. The elbows should be underneath or slightly ahead of the bar in the starting position. All repetitions begin from this starting position.
summary: Provides instructions on setting up for the push press.

content: While keeping the torso erect and head in a neutral position, flex the hips and knees at a slow to moderate speed to move the bar in a straight downward path during the dip phase. Do not change the position of the arms during the dip phase. The hips should not move backward during the dip phase; instead, they should remain directly underneath the shoulders.
summary: Describes the dip phase of an unspecified exercise.

content: Slowly lower the bar down to the thighs, slightly flexing the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs. If additional repetitions are to be performed, stand fully erect first and then follow the guidelines to move the body into the correct starting position. The bar does not return to the floor between repetitions. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Describes the lowering and reset procedures for an unspecified exercise, likely a squat variation.

content: The elbows should be underneath or slightly ahead of the bar in the starting position. All repetitions begin from this starting position. The elbows should be underneath or slightly ahead of the bar. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the push press: elbows under or slightly ahead of the bar.

content: While keeping the torso erect and head in a neutral position, flex the hips and knees at a slow to moderate speed to move the bar in a straight downward path during the dip phase. Do not change the position of the arms during the dip phase. The hips should not move backward during the dip phase; instead, they should remain directly underneath the shoulders. The downward movement of the dip phase is not a full squat but rather a dip to a depth not to exceed a quarter squat or the catch position of the power clean. Another guideline for the dip depth is that it should not exceed 10% of the lifter’s body height.
summary: Dip phase of the push press: controlled descent, torso erect, hips under shoulders, depth limited.

content: Immediately upon reaching the lowest position of the dip, reverse the movement by rapidly extending the hips, knees, and ankles to move the bar overhead during the upward movement phase. Initially, the bar needs to be held in place on the shoulders to benefit maximally from the upward momentum produced by the triple extension. Note that the heels may come off the floor as the bar leaves the shoulders during the upward movement phase. The neck must slightly hyperextend to allow the bar to pass by the chin during the upward movement phase.
summary: Upward movement phase of the push press: rapid extension, triple extension, bar held on shoulders, slight neck hyperextension.

content: Once the bar is overhead, establish the following positions: fully extended elbows, erect, stable torso, neutral head position, flat feet, and bar over or slightly behind the ears. Balance the body’s weight over the middle of the feet. Stand in a fully erect body position to gain control of the bar and achieve balance after reaching the overhead position.
summary: Overhead position: full extension, stable torso, balanced weight, erect posture.

content: At the completion of the repetition, lower the bar by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the arms to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the shoulders during the downward movement phase. The hips and knees are simultaneously flexed to cushion the impact of the bar on the shoulders during the downward movement phase. If additional repetitions are to be performed, stand fully erect first and then follow the guidelines described for the dip phase. Do not return the bar to the floor or rack between repetitions.
summary: Downward movement: controlled descent, hips and knees flexed, no returning bar to rack between reps.

content: At the completion of the set, first lower the bar from the shoulders to the thighs, then to the floor (similar to the power clean exercise). The bar can also be placed back on the power or squat rack.
summary: End of set: controlled descent to thighs and floor, or rack.

content: The push jerk exercise consists of quickly and forcefully flexing and then extending the hips and knees to drive the bar upward from the shoulders while pushing the body underneath. The push jerk exercise requires a rapid hip and knee extension to accelerate the bar off the shoulders. The push jerk also involves a forceful hip and knee thrust so that the bar is actually thrown (or jerked) upward, then caught with extended elbows in the overhead position with the hips and knees slightly flexed.
summary: Push jerk: rapid hip and knee extension, bar thrown upward, caught overhead with extended elbows.

content: To begin the push jerk, use the power clean or hang power clean exercise to lift the bar from the floor to the shoulders or remove the bar from a shoulder-height position on a power or squat rack. Stand erect with the feet hip- to shoulder-width apart and the toes pointed forward or slightly outward. Once the bar is positioned at the front of the shoulders, check to be sure that the grip on the bar is even, pronated, closed, and slightly wider than shoulder-width. The grip should remain relaxed on the bar.
summary: Starting the push jerk: power clean setup, erect stance, even grip.

content: The split jerk exercise consists of quickly and forcefully flexing and then extending the hips and knees to drive the bar upward from the shoulders while pushing the body underneath. The bar finishes directly overhead with the legs split into a lunge position. The split jerk exercise requires a rapid hip and knee extension to accelerate the bar off the shoulders. The split jerk also involves a forceful hip and knee thrust so that the bar is actually thrown (or jerked) upward, and then caught with extended elbows in the overhead position with the legs split into a lunge position.
summary: The split jerk is a complex exercise involving a rapid hip and knee thrust to propel the bar overhead, ending in a lunge position.

content: The power or hang power clean exercise is used to lift the bar from the floor to the shoulders or remove the bar from a shoulder-height position on a power or squat rack. Stand erect with the feet hip-width apart and the toes pointed forward or slightly outward. Once the bar is positioned at the front of the shoulders, check to be sure that the grip on the bar is even, pronated, closed, and slightly wider than shoulder-width. The grip should remain relaxed on the bar. The elbows should be underneath or slightly ahead of the bar. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: The power or hang power clean lifts the bar from the floor to the shoulders, starting with an erect stance and proper grip.

content: While keeping the torso erect and head in line with the spine, flex the hips and knees at a slow to moderate speed to move the bar in a straight downward path. Do not change the position of the arms. The hips should not move backward on the dip. Instead, they should remain directly underneath the shoulders. The downward movement is not a full squat, but rather a dip to a depth not to exceed a quarter squat or the catch position of the power clean. Another guideline is a depth that does not exceed 10% of the lifter’s body height.
summary: The power clean descent involves a controlled movement, keeping the torso erect and hips directly under the shoulders, stopping at a depth not exceeding a quarter squat.

content: Immediately upon reaching the lowest position of the dip, reverse the movement by rapidly extending the hips, knees, ankles, and then the elbows to move the bar overhead. Initially, the bar needs to be held in place on the shoulders to benefit maximally from the upward momentum produced by the triple extension. To reach the split position, rapidly move the feet forward and backward simultaneously, with some separation for balance side to side. The neck must slightly hyperextend to allow the bar to pass by the chin (or else the bar will hit the face).
summary: The power clean drive phase involves a rapid extension of the hips, knees, and elbows, using the triple extension for upward momentum.

content: The drive phase of the split jerk allows the bar to be caught over the head with the elbows fully extended and the legs in the lunge (split) position. Catch the bar by rapidly splitting the legs into a lunge position and pushing the body underneath as the bar is still rising from the drive phase. The goal is to catch the bar at the same moment the bar reaches maximal height and the feet come back into contact with the floor.
summary: The split jerk catch involves rapidly splitting the legs into a lunge position and pushing the body underneath as the bar is caught overhead.

content: At the completion of the repetition, lower the bar by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the arms to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the shoulders. The hips and knees are simultaneously flexed to cushion the impact of the bar on the shoulders. If additional repetitions are to be performed, stand fully erect first and then follow the guidelines described for the dip phase. The bar does not return to the floor or rack between repetitions.
summary: The completion of a repetition involves a controlled descent of the bar to the shoulders, followed by a controlled stand for the next repetition.

content: At the completion of the set, first lower the bar from the shoulders to the thighs, then to the floor (similar to the power clean exercise). The bar can also be placed back on the power or squat rack.
summary: At the end of a set, the bar is lowered to the thighs and then the floor, or placed back on the rack.

content: To reach the split position, rapidly move the feet forward and backward simultaneously, with some separation for balance side to side. Upon regaining contact with the floor, the front foot is flat and the heel of the back foot is up off the floor. The shin of the front leg and the thigh of the back leg should be nearly perpendicular to the floor. The proper length of the split and foot positioning can be determined by these angles.  The feet should come parallel to meet in the middle of the distance of the split.  Hold the bar in its final position directly above the head, shoulders, hips, knees, and ankles, all of which are vertically aligned.
summary: Describes the split position and foot placement during the split jerk.

content: The neck must slightly hyperextend to allow the bar to pass by the chin (or else the bar will hit the face). The torso should be erect, with the head in line with the spine directly under the bar; the eyes are focused forward.
summary: Details the proper neck and torso positioning during the split jerk.

content: The drive phase of the split jerk allows the bar to be caught over the head with the elbows fully extended and the legs in the lunge (split) position. Catch the bar by rapidly splitting the legs into a lunge position and pushing the body underneath as the bar is still rising from the drive phase. The goal is to catch the bar at the same moment the bar reaches maximal height and the feet come back into contact with the floor. In comparison to the starting position, the feet move into a slightly wider stance while in the lunge position when coming back into contact with the floor.
summary: Explains the drive phase and bar catch technique in the split jerk.

content: After gaining control and balance, stand erect by stepping backward half the distance of the split with the front foot, and then stepping forward half the distance of the split with the back foot.  If additional repetitions are to be performed, stand fully erect first and then follow the guidelines described for the dip phase. Do not return the bar to the floor between repetitions.
summary: Outlines the steps for regaining balance and preparing for subsequent repetitions in the split jerk.

content: At the completion of the repetition, lower the bar by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the arms to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the shoulders. The hips and knees are simultaneously flexed to cushion the impact of the bar on the shoulders. At the completion of the set, first lower the bar from the shoulders to the thighs, then to the floor (similar to the power clean exercise). The bar can also be placed back on the power or squat rack.
summary: Describes the descent and end-of-set procedures for the split jerk.

content: With the bar positioned at approximately armpit height on the supporting pins or ledge of a shoulder-high rack stand (or in a power or squat rack), move toward the bar and position the front of the shoulders, hips, and feet directly under the bar. The most common hand/arm position for the front squat is the clean or parallel-arm position: grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Rotate the arms around the bar to place the bar on top of the anterior deltoids and clavicles. The backs of the hands should be either slightly on top of or just outside of the shoulders, right next to where the bar rests on the deltoids.
summary: Provides instructions for setting up for the front squat.

content: An alternative hand position is the crossed-arm position: flex the elbows and cross the arms in front of the chest. Move the body to position the bar evenly on the anterior deltoids without touching it with the hands. After the body is in the correct position, place the hands on top of the bar and use pressure from the fingers to keep it in position. Note that this is an open grip; the thumb will not be able to encircle the bar because the shoulders will be in the way. Raise the elbows to lift the upper arms to a position parallel to the floor. For either hand position, keep the elbows lifted up and forward. This contributes greatly to securing the bar on the shoulders.
summary: Describes alternative hand positions for the front squat.

content: Signal the spotters for assistance, extend the hips and knees to lift the bar off the supporting pins or ledge, and then take a step backward. Be aware of the frame of the rack. If performing the front squat on the inside of a four-pole, boxed type of rack, there may be only about 12 to 18 inches (30 to 46 cm) of space for the backward step. Leave enough room (front to back) so that the bar will not strike the frame during the exercise.
summary: Details the initial lift and safety considerations for the front squat.

content: Position the feet between hip-and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. Stand with an erect torso by positioning the shoulders back, tilting the head slightly back, and protruding the chest up and out to create a neutral or slightly arched back. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting stance and posture for the front squat.

content: Stand erect at opposite ends of the bar with the feet shoulder-width apart and the knees slightly flexed. Grasp the end of the bar by wrapping the hands around the bar with the thumbs crossed and palms facing the barbell. At the lifter’s signal, assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved off the supporting pins or ledge. Move sideways in unison with the lifter as the lifter steps backward. Release the bar smoothly. Hold the hands 2 to 3 inches (5 to 8 cm) below the ends of the bar.
summary: Provides instructions for spotters assisting with the front squat.

content: Grasp the end of the bar by wrapping the hands around the bar with the thumbs crossed and palms facing the barbell. Hold the hands 2 to 3 inches (5 to 8 cm) below the ends of the bar.  With the bar positioned at approximately armpit height on the supporting pins or ledge of a shoulder-high rack stand (or in a power or squat rack), move toward the bar and position the base of the neck (or upper midback), the hips, and the feet directly under the bar. To perform the back squat with a low bar placement, do the following: Place the bar evenly on top of the posterior deltoids at the middle of the trapezius. Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, wider than shoulder-width apart. For most people, the hand placement is wide to compensate for the lower bar position. An alternative grip for the back squat is an open grip, which may be more comfortable for the wrists. An open grip does not provide as much control of the bar as a closed grip. To perform the back squat with a high bar placement, place the bar evenly above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck. Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Raise the elbows to create a shelf with the upper back and shoulder muscles for the bar to rest on. A high elbow position allows the arms to maintain pressure on the bar to prevent it from sliding down the back.
summary: This chunk describes the proper grip and bar placement for both low and high bar back squats.

content: At the lifter’s signal, assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved off the supporting pins or ledge. After the lifter is in position, assume a shoulder-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect. Begin the exercise by flexing the hips and knees slowly and under control. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and rigid-arm position; do not round the upper back or lean forward as the bar is lowered. Focus the eyes ahead and slightly above horizontal, and tilt the head back slightly. Keep the body’s weight over the middle and heel area of the feet; do not allow the heels to rise off the floor during the descent. As the knees flex, keep them aligned over the feet. Continue the downward movement until one of these three events occurs (they determine the maximum range of motion, or the lowest squat position): 1. The thighs are parallel to the floor (if achievable). 2. The torso begins to round or flex forward. 3. The heels rise off the floor. Actual squat depth is dependent on lower body joint flexibility. Keep the body tight and in control; do not bounce, or relax the legs or torso at the bottom of the movement. Keep the thumbs crossed and hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. Slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar.
summary: This chunk details the initial setup and descent phase of the back squat.

content: Raise the bar under control by extending the hips and knees. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and rigid-arm position. As the bar is raised, resist the tendency to lean forward by keeping the head tilted slightly back and the chest held up and out. Move the bar upward by pushing up through the whole foot with the body’s weight evenly distributed between the heel and midfoot to keep each foot in contact with the floor and the hips under the bar. Do not allow the body’s weight to shift forward onto the balls of the feet. Keep the knees aligned over the feet; do not allow the knees to shift inward or outward as they extend. Continue raising the bar at an even rate until the hips and the knees are fully extended to return to the starting position. At the completion of the set, signal the spotters for assistance to rack the bar, but keep a grip on the bar until both ends are secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge. Keep the thumbs crossed and hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. Slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar.
summary: This chunk describes the upward movement and completion of the back squat.

content: At the lifter’s signal after the set is completed, move sideways in unison with the lifter back to the rack. Simultaneously grasp the bar and assist with balancing the bar as it is placed back on the supporting pins or ledge. Release the bar smoothly. There may be only about 12 to 18 inches (30 to 46 cm) of space for the backward step when performing the back squat on the inside of a four-pole, boxed type of rack. Leave enough room (front to back) so that the bar will not strike the frame during the exercise.
summary: This chunk outlines the final steps of the back squat, including returning to the rack and safety considerations.

content: Raise the elbows to create a shelf with the upper back and shoulder muscles for the bar to rest on. A high elbow position allows the arms to maintain pressure on the bar to prevent it from sliding down the back.
summary: Proper elbow positioning in the back squat.

content: Signal the spotters for assistance, extend the hips and knees to lift the bar off the supporting pins or ledge, and then take a step backward. There may be only about 12 to 18 inches (30 to 46 cm) of space for the backward step when performing the back squat on the inside of a four-pole, boxed type of rack. Leave enough room (front to back) so that the bar will not strike the frame during the exercise.
summary: Initiating the back squat and safety considerations.

content: Position the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. Stand with an erect torso by positioning the shoulders back, tilting the head slightly back, and protruding the chest up and out to create a neutral or slightly arched back. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Foot placement and torso positioning for the back squat.
