content: Two spotters stand erect at opposite ends of the bar with the feet shoulder-width apart and the knees slightly flexed. Grasp the end of the bar by wrapping the hands around the bar with the thumbs crossed and palms facing the barbell. After the lifter is in position, assume a shoulder-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect.
summary: Spotter positioning and grip for the back squat.

content: At the lifter’s signal, assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved off the supporting pins or ledge. Move sideways in unison with the lifter as the lifter steps backward. Release the bar smoothly.
summary: Spotter actions during the back squat lift.

content: Begin the exercise by flexing the hips and knees slowly and under control. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and high elbow position; do not round the upper back or lean forward as the bar is lowered. Focus the eyes ahead and slightly above horizontal, and tilt the head back slightly. Keep the body’s weight over the middle and heel area of the feet; do not allow the heels to rise off the floor during the descent. Keep the knees aligned over the feet as the knees flex.
summary: Initiating the descent in the back squat.

content: Continue the downward movement until one of these three events occurs (they determine the maximum range of motion, or the lowest squat position): 1. The thighs are parallel to the floor (if achievable). 2. The torso begins to round or flex forward. 3. The heels rise off the floor. Actual squat depth is dependent on lower body joint flexibility.
summary: Determining squat depth.

content: Keep the body tight and in control; do not bounce, or relax the legs or torso at the bottom of the movement. Keep the thumbs crossed and hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends.
summary: Maintaining control at the bottom of the squat.

content: Raise the bar under control by extending the hips and knees. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and high elbow position. As the bar is raised, resist the tendency to lean forward by keeping the head tilted slightly back and the chest up and out. Move the bar upward by pushing up through the whole foot with the body’s weight evenly distributed between the heels and midfoot to keep the feet in contact with the floor and the hips under the bar. Do not allow the body’s weight to shift forward onto the balls of the feet.
summary: Ascending from the bottom of the squat.

content: Keep the knees aligned over the feet; do not allow the knees to shift inward or outward as they extend. Continue raising the bar at an even rate until the hips and knees are fully extended to return to the starting position.
summary: Knee alignment and completing the squat.

content: At the completion of the set, signal the spotters for assistance to rack the bar, but keep a grip on the bar until both ends are secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge. Keep the thumbs crossed and hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. Slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar.
summary: Racking the bar and final actions.

content: At the lifter’s signal after the set is completed, move sideways in unison with the lifter’s back to the rack. Simultaneously grasp the bar and assist with balancing the bar as it is placed back on the supporting pins or ledge. Release the bar smoothly.
summary: Spotter actions after the set.

content: The Romanian Deadlift (RDL) starting position is identical to the deadlift starting position, but with knees slightly flexed and remaining in that position throughout the movement. The RDL downward movement begins with slowly lowering the bar while keeping it as close to the body as possible.
summary: Starting position and initial movement of the Romanian Deadlift.

content: The starting position for the exercise involves a closed grip with the hands shoulder-width apart or slightly wider. The feet should be positioned between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward so the knees track directly over the feet. The arms should be positioned outside of the knees with the elbows fully extended and pointing out to the sides. The bar should be positioned approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. All repetitions begin from this starting position.
summary: Describes the starting position for a barbell exercise, emphasizing grip, foot placement, and arm positioning.

content: The upward movement begins by extending the knees and hips slowly and under control. The torso angle should remain constant in relation to the floor during the upward movement. The hips should not rise before or faster than the shoulders during the ascent. The back should be kept neutral or slightly arched during the upward movement. Full elbow extension should be maintained during the ascent with the head neutral in relation to the spine. The shoulders should be positioned over or slightly ahead of the bar during the ascent. During the ascent, the bar should be kept as close to the shins as possible. As the bar rises to just above the knees, the body's weight should shift back toward the heels. Once the bar rises to just above the knees, shift the body’s weight forward toward the balls of the feet, keeping the heels on the floor. Continue to extend the hips and knees until the body reaches a fully erect torso position.
summary: Details the proper technique for the upward movement of a barbell exercise, emphasizing controlled movement, torso angle, and weight distribution.

content: The downward movement involves slowly flexing the hips and knees at the same rate to return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner. During the descent, the bar should be kept as close to the thighs and shins as possible. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back, with the elbows fully extended and pointing out to the sides, the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar, and the head neutral in relation to the spine during the descent. Touch the plates to the floor, and then immediately (without a pause) lift the bar back up for the next repetition.
summary: Outlines the correct technique for the downward movement of a barbell exercise, focusing on controlled flexion, bar position, and maintaining a neutral spine.

content: The Romanian Deadlift (RDL) starting position is identical to the deadlift starting position, but with knees slightly flexed and remaining in that position throughout the movement. The RDL downward movement begins with slowly lowering the bar while keeping it as close to the body as possible. During the RDL downward movement, the hips flex and move backward while the knees remain slightly flexed. The shoulders move forward as the hips move backward and remain directly over or in front of the bar during the RDL downward movement. The foot's balance center starts in the midfoot and moves toward the heel as the bar is lowered during the RDL. The RDL downward movement continues until the bar reaches just below the knees or the tibial tuberosity, whichever point prevents maintaining a neutral spine. The RDL upward movement begins with extending the hips while keeping the bar as close to the body as possible. The hips move forward as they extend during the RDL upward movement, while the shoulders move backward as the torso moves toward the vertical position. The foot's balance center starts in the heel and moves toward the midfoot as the bar is raised during the RDL upward movement. The RDL upward movement continues until the body reaches the starting position.
summary: Describes the Romanian Deadlift (RDL) technique, highlighting the starting position, downward and upward movements, and key body positioning.

content: To start the hip sled exercise, sit inside the machine with the head, back, hips, and buttocks pressed evenly against their respective pads. All body segments other than the legs must be firmly positioned against their pads to provide maximal support to the spine and low back. If the angle of the back pad is adjustable, move it up or down to allow the torso and legs to form approximately a 90-degree angle (at the hips) when the feet are properly positioned on the foot platform and the knees are fully extended. Place the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart and flat on the foot platform with the toes pointed slightly outward. Both feet should have an identical toed-out angle. Position the thighs and lower legs parallel to each other. Grasp the handles or the machine frame and simultaneously extend the hips and knees to raise the foot platform 1 to 2 inches (3 to 5 cm). Keep the hips and buttocks on the seat and the back pressed firmly and evenly against the back pad as the foot platform rises.
summary: Provides instructions for setting up and initiating the hip sled exercise, emphasizing proper body positioning and support.

content: At the lifter’s signal after the set is completed, move sideways in unison with the lifter’s back to the rack. Simultaneously grasp the bar and assist with balancing the bar as it is placed back on the supporting pins or ledge. Release the bar smoothly.
summary: Describes the actions of a spotter during a barbell exercise, focusing on safety and assistance.

content: Place the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart and flat on the foot platform with the toes pointed slightly outward. Both feet should have an identical toed-out angle. Position the thighs and lower legs parallel to each other. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for a leg press exercise, emphasizing foot placement, angle, and leg alignment.

content: Begin the exercise by flexing the hips and knees slowly and under control. Keep the hips and buttocks on the seat and the back pressed firmly and evenly against the back pad. Keep the thighs and lower legs parallel to each other; any deviations will place undue stress on the low back and knees. Keep the knees aligned over the feet as they flex. The extent of the range of motion depends on the degree of spinal, hip, knee, and ankle flexibility as well as the machine’s design features and adjustment capabilities. At the bottom of the movement, do not relax the legs or torso, and do not bounce the foot platform to spring it back up for the next repetition.
summary: Details the eccentric (lowering) phase of the leg press, emphasizing controlled movement, proper form, and avoiding common mistakes.

content: Push the foot platform up and under control by extending the hips and knees. The feet should remain flat on the foot platform. Keep the hips and buttocks on the seat and the back pressed firmly and evenly against the back pad. Do not shift the hips or allow the buttocks to lose contact with the seat. Keep the thighs and lower legs parallel to each other; do not allow the knees to shift inward or outward as they extend. Continue pushing the foot platform up until the knees are fully extended but not forcefully locked.
summary: Describes the concentric (lifting) phase of the leg press, emphasizing controlled movement, proper form, and avoiding overextension.

content: At the completion of the set, slightly flex the hips and knees, return the support mechanism back into place, lower the foot platform until it is resting on the supports, and then stand up and step out of the machine.
summary: Outlines the final steps of the leg press exercise, including returning to the starting position and exiting the machine safely.

content: Sit in the machine with the head, back, hips, and buttocks pressed evenly against their respective pads (i.e., in the center of the pads, not to the left or right side). All body segments other than the legs must be firmly positioned against their pads to provide maximal support to the spine and low back.
summary: Provides instructions for proper seating and body positioning on a leg press machine to ensure spinal support and safety.

content: If the horizontal position of the foot platform or the seat is adjustable, move it forward or backward to allow the thighs to be parallel to the foot platform when sitting in the starting position. Place the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart and flat on the foot platform with the toes pointed slightly outward. Both feet should have an identical toed-out angle. The legs should be positioned parallel to each other. Grasp the handles or the sides of the seat.
summary: Details adjustments and starting position for a leg press exercise, emphasizing foot placement, angle, and leg alignment.

content: Begin the exercise by extending the hips and knees slowly and under control to push the platform forward. The feet should remain flat on the foot platform. Keep the head, shoulders, back, hips, and buttocks pressed evenly against their respective pads; do not shift the hips or allow the buttocks to lose contact with the seat. Keep the legs parallel to each other; do not allow the knees to shift inward or outward as they extend. Continue the forward movement until the knees are fully extended but not forcefully locked.
summary: Describes the concentric (lifting) phase of a leg press variation, emphasizing controlled movement, proper form, and avoiding overextension.

content: Allow the hips and knees to flex slowly to bring the platform back to the starting position. Keep the head, shoulders, back, hips, and buttocks pressed evenly against their respective pads. Keep the legs parallel to each other; any deviations will place undue stress on the low back and knees. As the knees flex, keep them aligned over the feet. Continue flexing the hips and knees until the thighs are parallel to the foot platform.
summary: Details the eccentric (lowering) phase of a leg press variation, emphasizing controlled movement, proper form, and avoiding common mistakes.

content: At the completion of the set, release the handles or the sides of the seat and step out of the machine.
summary: Outlines the final steps of a leg press variation, including returning to the starting position and exiting the machine safely.

content: Keep the head, shoulders, back, hips, and buttocks pressed evenly against their respective pads. Keep the legs parallel to each other; any deviations will place undue stress on the low back and knees. As the knees flex, keep them aligned over the feet. Continue flexing the hips and knees until the thighs are parallel to the foot platform. At the completion of the set, release the handles or the sides of the seat and step out of the machine.
summary: Instructions for maintaining proper form during a seated leg press exercise.

content: The box used for this exercise should have a top surface area that allows the lifter’s whole foot (shoe) to fit with extra space behind the heel and ahead of the toe. The box should be 12 to 18 inches (30 to 46 cm) high, or high enough to create a 90-degree angle at the knee and hip joints when the lead foot is on the box. The box should be placed on a nonslip floor and have a nonslip top surface.
summary: Specifications for the box used in a step-up exercise.

content: With the bar positioned at approximately armpit height on the outside of a power or squat rack, move toward the bar and position the base of the neck (or upper midback) and the hips and feet directly under the bar. Place the bar evenly above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck (as seen in the high bar position in the back squat exercise). Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Raise the elbows to create a shelf with the upper back and shoulder muscles for the bar to rest on (a high elbow position also allows the arms to maintain pressure on the bar to prevent it from sliding down the back).
summary: Instructions for setting up for a barbell exercise, likely a squat variation.

content: Signal the spotter for assistance and then extend the hips and knees to lift the bar off the supporting pins or ledge. Move to a spot near the front of the box. Place the feet hip-width apart with the toes pointed ahead. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting the lift after setting up for a barbell exercise.

content: Stand erect and close behind the lifter (but not so close as to be a distraction). Place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. At the lifter’s signal, assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved out of the rack. Move in unison with the lifter as the lifter moves to the starting position.
summary: Instructions for a spotter during a barbell exercise.

content: After the lifter is in position, assume a hip-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect. Position the hands near the lifter’s hips, waist, or torso.
summary: Spotter positioning for a barbell exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by stepping up with one leg (the lead leg). The initial contact of the lead foot with the top of the box must be made by the entire foot; do not allow the heel to hang off the edge of the box. Keep the torso erect; do not lean forward. Keep the trailing foot in the starting position, but shift the body’s weight to the lead leg. Forcefully extend the lead hip and knee to move the body up and on top of the box; do not push off or hop up with the trailing leg or foot.
summary: Instructions for the first half of a step-up exercise.

content: As the hip and knee of the lead leg fully extend to a standing position on top of the box, bring the trailing foot up and place it next to the lead foot. At the highest position, stand erect and pause before beginning the downward movement.
summary: Completing the upward phase of a step-up exercise.

content: Take a small step forward with the lead leg as the lifter steps up on the box. As the lifter reaches the highest position, bring the trailing leg forward to be next to the lead leg.
summary: Spotter guidance during the upward phase of a step-up exercise.

content: Keep the hands as near as possible to the lifter’s hips, waist, or torso. Assist only when necessary to keep the lifter balanced. Shift the body’s weight to the same lead leg. Step off the box with the same trailing leg. Maintain an erect torso position. Place the trailing foot on the floor the same distance from the box as the starting position. When the trailing foot is in full contact with the floor, shift the body’s weight to the trailing leg. Step off the box with the lead leg. Bring the lead foot back to a position next to the trailing foot.
summary: Instructions for the downward phase of a step-up exercise.

content: Stand erect in the starting position, pause to gain full balance, and then alternate lead legs and repeat the movement with the new lead leg.
summary: Completing a set of step-up exercises.

content: Stand erect in the starting position, pause to gain full balance, and then alternate lead legs and repeat the movement with the new lead leg. Some lifters may benefit from repeating the action words ‘up-up-down-down’ during the set to help perform the exercise correctly. At the completion of the set, signal the spotter for assistance to rack the bar, but keep a grip on the bar until both ends are secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge.
summary: Instructions for performing a stepping exercise, emphasizing balance and proper form.

content: Place the trailing foot on the floor the same distance from the box as the starting position. When the trailing foot is in full contact with the floor, shift the body’s weight to the trailing leg. Step off the box with the lead leg. Bring the lead foot back to a position next to the trailing foot.
summary: Steps for executing a single repetition of a stepping exercise from a box.

content: Take a small step backward with the same trailing leg as the lifter steps back down to the floor. As the lifter steps off the box with the lead leg, take a step backward with the same lead leg.
summary: Spotter's movements during a stepping exercise from a box.

content: Stand erect in the starting position, pause to wait for the lifter, and alternate lead legs. Assist only when necessary to keep the lifter balanced. At the lifter’s signal after the set is completed, help the lifter rack the bar.
summary: Spotter's role during a stepping exercise from a box.

content: This exercise can be performed in a variety of ways in many directions. For many people, performing this exercise with only the body as the weight is enough. Well-trained lifters can use a bar (as explained in the following section) for additional resistance. An alternative is to hold a pair of dumbbells at the sides; this is especially helpful if balancing a bar across the shoulders is too difficult or if an experienced spotter is not available.
summary: Variations and modifications for a stepping exercise.

content: In any situation, a large (or at least long) floor space is required for this exercise.
summary: Space requirements for a stepping exercise.

content: With the bar positioned at approximately armpit height on the outside of a power or squat rack, move toward the bar and position the base of the neck (or upper midback) and the hips and feet directly under the bar. Place the bar evenly above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck (as seen in the high bar position in the back squat exercise).
summary: Setting up for a barbell stepping exercise.

content: Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Raise the elbows to create a shelf with the upper back and shoulder muscles for the bar to rest on (a high elbow position also allows the arms to maintain pressure on the bar to prevent it from sliding down the back).
summary: Grip and elbow position for a barbell stepping exercise.

content: Signal the spotter for assistance, and then extend the hips and knees to lift the bar off the supporting pins or ledge. Take two or three steps backward.
summary: Initiating the barbell stepping exercise.

content: Place the feet hip-width apart with the toes pointed ahead. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for a barbell stepping exercise.

content: Stand erect and close behind the lifter (but not close enough to be a distraction). Place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed.
summary: Spotter's position for a barbell stepping exercise.

content: After the lifter is in position, assume a hip-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect. Position the hands near the lifter’s hips, waist, or torso.
summary: Spotter's stance for a barbell stepping exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by taking one exaggerated step directly forward with one leg; this leg is called the lead leg. Keep the torso erect and arms tight as the lead foot moves forward and contacts the floor. The trailing foot remains in its starting position, but as the lead leg steps forward, balance shifts to the ball of the trailing foot and the trailing knee flexes slightly.
summary: Initiating a lunge exercise.

content: Plant the lead foot flat on the floor with the toes pointing ahead or slightly inward. To help maintain balance, this foot needs to be placed directly ahead from its initial position, and the lead ankle, knee, and hip must be in one vertical plane. Do not step slightly to the right or left, or allow the knee to shift in or out.
summary: Proper foot placement and alignment for a lunge exercise.

content: After balance has shifted to both feet and stability is achieved, flex the lead knee to lower the trailing knee toward the floor. The torso must stay erect, with the shoulders held back and the head facing forward. Sit back on the trailing leg; do not lean forward or look down.
summary: Lowering phase of a lunge exercise.

content: The lowest ideal body position is with the trailing knee 1 to 2 inches (3 to 5 cm) away from the floor, the lead knee flexed to about 90 degrees, the lead lower leg perpendicular to the floor, and the lead foot flat on the floor. The lead knee must not extend past the toes of the lead foot. Actual lunge depth depends primarily on hip joint flexibility, especially in the iliopsoas muscles.
summary: Ideal depth and alignment for a lunge exercise.

content: Allow the ankle of the trailing foot to fully dorsiflex with the toes fully extended.
summary: Ankle position during a lunge exercise.

content: Step forward with the same lead leg as the lifter. Keep the lead knee and foot aligned with the lifter’s lead knee and foot. Plant the lead foot 12 to 18 inches (30 to 46 cm) behind the lifter’s lead foot.
summary: Steps for the next repetition of a lunge exercise.

content: The lowest ideal body position is with the trailing knee 1 to 2 inches (3 to 5 cm) away from the floor, the lead knee flexed to about 90 degrees, the lead lower leg perpendicular to the floor, and the lead foot flat on the floor. The lead knee must not extend past the toes of the lead foot. Actual lunge depth depends primarily on hip joint flexibility, especially in the iliopsoas muscles. Allow the ankle of the trailing foot to fully dorsiflex with the toes fully extended.
summary: Describes the ideal body position during a lunge, emphasizing knee and ankle alignment and the influence of hip flexibility.

content: Step forward with the same lead leg as the lifter. Plant the lead foot 12 to 18 inches (30 to 46 cm) behind the lifter’s lead foot. Flex the lead knee as the lifter’s lead knee flexes. Keep the lead knee and foot aligned with the lifter’s lead knee and foot.
summary: Outlines the initial steps of a lunge, focusing on foot placement and knee alignment.

content: Keep the torso erect. Keep the hands near the lifter’s hips, waist, or torso. Assist only when necessary to keep the lifter balanced.
summary: Provides instructions on maintaining proper posture and providing assistance during a lunge.

content: Shift the balance forward to the lead foot and forcefully push off the floor with the lead foot by plantar flexing the ankle of the lead foot and by extending the lead knee and hip joints. Do not jerk the upper body back; maintain its vertical position. As the lead foot moves back toward the trailing foot, balance will shift back to the trailing foot. This will cause the heel of the trailing foot to regain contact with the floor.
summary: Describes the upward phase of a lunge, emphasizing proper foot and joint movement and maintaining an upright torso.

content: Move the lead foot back to place it next to the trailing foot. Do not stutter-step backward. As the lead foot is placed flat on the floor in its starting position, evenly divide the body’s weight over both feet. The torso should be erect, similar to the starting position.
summary: Details the final steps of a lunge, ensuring smooth transition and proper weight distribution.

content: Stand erect in the starting position, pause to gain full balance, and then alternate lead legs and repeat the movement with the new lead leg.
summary: Provides instructions on completing a set of lunges, alternating legs and maintaining balance.

content: Some lifters may benefit from dividing the movement into smaller portions during the repetition to help perform the exercise correctly: 1. Step forward. 2. Plant the lead foot. 3. Lunge down and back. 4. Stand up. 5. Push off.
summary: Offers a breakdown of the lunge movement into smaller steps for easier execution.

content: To begin a Glute Ham Raise, position the body on the glute ham bench with the ankles between the ankle roller pads, the feet flat against the foot platform, and the thighs (just above the knees) pressed against the pad. Flex the knees to approximately 90 degrees to position the upper body perpendicular to the floor. The shoulders, hips, and knees should be vertically aligned with the head in a neutral position during the starting position of the Glute Ham Raise. Cross the arms in front of the chest in the starting position of the Glute Ham Raise.
summary: Describes the starting position for a Glute Ham Raise, emphasizing proper body alignment and hand placement.

content: Begin the downward movement of the Glute Ham Raise by slowly allowing the knees to extend. The shoulders, hips, and knees remain aligned with the arms still crossed in front of the chest during the downward movement of the Glute Ham Raise.
summary: Outlines the initial phase of the Glute Ham Raise, focusing on controlled knee extension and maintaining body alignment.

content: As the aligned torso and thighs approach parallel to the floor during the Glute Ham Raise, allow the hips to flex and the knees to fully extend. At the lowest point of the Glute Ham Raise, the shoulders and hips are still aligned with the head in a neutral position.
summary: Describes the continuation of the Glute Ham Raise, detailing hip flexion and knee extension as the body approaches parallel to the floor.

content: Begin the upward movement of the Glute Ham Raise by extending the hips. Maintain alignment of the hips and shoulders during the upward movement of the Glute Ham Raise. When the torso and thighs are parallel to the floor during the Glute Ham Raise, flex the knees to allow the thighs to move down on the thigh pad so the hips can extend over the top of the pad.
summary: Explains the upward phase of the Glute Ham Raise, emphasizing hip extension and maintaining alignment.

content: Continue extending the hips and flexing the knees to approximately a 90-degree angle to move the upper body back to the starting position during the Glute Ham Raise.
summary: Completes the Glute Ham Raise, detailing the final movement to return to the starting position.

content: Well-trained lifters may stand on a raised platform to perform the Stiff-Leg Deadlift through a greater range of motion.
summary: Notes a variation for experienced lifters to increase the range of motion during a Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: Begin the upward movement of the Glute Ham Raise by extending the hips. Maintain alignment of the hips and shoulders during the upward movement of the Glute Ham Raise. When the torso and thighs are parallel to the floor during the Glute Ham Raise, flex the knees to allow the thighs to move down on the thigh pad so the hips can extend over the top of the pad. Continue extending the hips and flexing the knees to approximately a 90-degree angle to move the upper body back to the starting position during the Glute Ham Raise.
summary: Describes the proper technique for performing a Glute Ham Raise.

content: Well-trained lifters may stand on a raised platform to perform the Stiff-Leg Deadlift through a greater range of motion. Instead of touching the plates to the floor, the lifter can lower the bar to touch the feet during the Stiff-Leg Deadlift. Doing so requires an extremely large degree of flexibility in the hamstrings, gluteals, and low back; therefore, most people should not use a raised platform for the Stiff-Leg Deadlift. Nearly all lifters should stand on the floor and safely lower the bar only to knee or midshin level during the Stiff-Leg Deadlift.
summary: Provides guidance on variations and safety considerations for the Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: Observe the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to place the body in the correct position to lift the bar off the floor for the Stiff-Leg Deadlift. Follow the starting position and upward movement guidelines of the deadlift exercise to get in the correct starting position of the Stiff-Leg Deadlift, but with one important exception: The knees are slightly flexed and remain in this position throughout the downward and upward movements. All repetitions of the Stiff-Leg Deadlift begin from this position. Begin the Stiff-Leg Deadlift exercise by forming a neutral or slightly arched back, and then flex forward at the hips slowly and under control.
summary: Outlines the starting position and initial movement for the Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: During the descent, keep the knees in the same slightly flexed position, with the back neutral or slightly arched and the elbows fully extended. Continue the downward movement until one of these four events occurs: the plates touch the floor (or the bar touches the feet of well-trained lifters standing on a raised platform), the back cannot be held in the neutral or slightly arched position, the knees fully extend, or the heels rise off the floor. Keep the body tight and in control; do not bounce or relax the torso at the bottom of the movement.
summary: Describes the descent phase of the Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: Raise the bar by extending the hips. During the ascent, keep the knees in the same slightly flexed position with the back neutral or slightly arched and the elbows fully extended. Continue the upward movement to return to the starting position. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to squat down and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Details the upward movement and end-of-set procedure for the Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: With the bar positioned at approximately armpit height on the outside of a power or squat rack, move toward the bar and position the base of the neck (or upper midback) and the hips and feet directly under the bar. Place the bar evenly above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck. Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Raise the elbows to create a shelf with the upper back and shoulder muscles for the bar to rest on (a high elbow position also allows the arms to maintain pressure on the bar to prevent it from sliding down the back). To remove the bar from the rack, extend the hips and knees to lift the bar off the supporting pins or ledge and take a few steps backward.
summary: Describes the setup and initial lift for a barbell exercise (likely a back squat or overhead press).

content: Position the body with the feet between hip- and shoulder-width apart, knees slightly flexed, toes angled slightly outward (about 10 degrees), torso erect with shoulders back, head tilted slightly back, chest up and out to create a neutral or slightly arched back, and elbows held up to keep the bar in position. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by flexing the hips slowly and under control. The buttocks should move straight back during the descent and the knees should remain slightly flexed. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and high elbow position. Focus the eyes ahead and slightly above horizontal, and tilt the head back. Do not allow the heels to rise off the floor.
summary: Details the starting position and initial movement for a barbell exercise (likely a back squat or overhead press).

content: Continue the downward movement until the torso is parallel to the floor. If unable to achieve the parallel position, continue the exercise only as far as proper technique is maintained. Keep the body tight and in control; do not bounce or relax the torso at the bottom of the movement. Raise the bar by extending the hips slowly and under control; keep the knees slightly flexed. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and high elbow position with the head tilted back. Continue the upward movement to return to the starting position. At the completion of the set, slowly walk forward and return the bar to the supporting pins or ledge.
summary: Describes the descent, ascent, and end-of-set procedure for a barbell exercise (likely a back squat or overhead press).

content: Leg (Knee) Extension (Machine) Starting Position: Sit erect on the seat with the back and hips pressed evenly against their respective pads. The position in the machine must allow the knees to be in line with the axis of rotation of the machine. If the back pad is adjustable, move it forward or backward to create this alignment. Position the thighs, lower legs, and feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other. Grasp the side handles or seat pad.
summary: Provides instructions for the starting position of the Leg Extension machine.

content: Leg (Knee) Extension (Machine) Starting Position: Sit erect on the seat with the back and hips pressed evenly against their respective pads. The position in the machine must allow the knees to be in line with the axis of rotation of the machine. If the back pad is adjustable, move it forward or backward to create this alignment. Position the thighs, lower legs, and feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other. Grasp the side handles or seat pad. All repetitions begin from this position. Upward Movement: Begin the exercise by extending the knees slowly and under control. Keep the thighs, lower legs, and feet parallel to each other; do not allow the thighs to shift inward or outward (i.e., internally or externally rotate at the hip) as the knees extend. Keep a tight grasp on the handles or seat pad during the ascent to minimize upper body and thigh movement. Do not swing the legs or jerk the torso backward to help raise the weight. Continue the upward movement until the knees are fully extended but not forcefully locked. Downward Movement: Allow the knees to flex to lower the roller pad slowly and under control back to the starting position. Do not lower the weight stack uncontrollably. Keep the thighs, lower legs, and feet parallel to each other. The back and hips should remain in contact with their respective pads. At the completion of the set, unhook the feet from under the roller pad and step out of the machine.
summary: Instructions for performing the Leg (Knee) Extension (Machine) exercise, covering starting position, upward and downward movements, and safety precautions.

content: The starting position for the Seated Leg (Knee) Curl (Machine) exercise requires the thigh pad to be raised to its highest position. The user should sit erect on the seat with the back and hips pressed evenly against their respective pads. The knees should be extended and the ankles placed on top of the roller pad. The roller pad should be positioned to be in contact with the back of the heels or lower calves, just above the tops of the shoes. The position in the machine must allow the knees to be in line with the axis of rotation of the machine. The back pad should be adjusted if necessary to create this alignment. The thighs, lower legs, and feet should be positioned hip-width apart and parallel to each other. The lower thigh pad should be lowered so it firmly presses against the thighs. The user should grasp the side handles or seat pad. All repetitions begin from this starting position. The downward movement of the Seated Leg (Knee) Curl (Machine) exercise begins with slowly flexing the knees under control. The thighs, lower legs, and feet should remain parallel to each other during the downward movement. The thighs should not shift inward or outward (i.e., internally or externally rotate at the hip) as the knees flex. The user should maintain a tight grasp on the handles or seat pad to minimize upper body and thigh movement. The upper body should not be moved or kicked back with the legs to help raise the weight. The downward movement should continue until the knees are flexed at least 90 degrees. The actual range of motion will depend on the length of the limbs, the flexibility of the quadriceps, and the design of the machine. The upward movement of the Seated Leg (Knee) Curl (Machine) exercise involves allowing the knees to extend to raise the roller pad slowly and under control back to the starting position. The weight stack should not be lowered uncontrollably. The thighs, lower legs, and feet should remain parallel to each other during the upward movement. The back, hips, and thighs should remain in contact with their respective pads. At the completion of the set, the thigh pad should be raised to the highest position, the heels should be moved off the roller pad, and the user should step out of the machine.
summary: Instructions for performing the Seated Leg (Knee) Curl (Machine) exercise, covering starting position, upward and downward movements, and safety precautions.

content: The starting position for the Seated Calf (Heel) Raise (Machine) exercise requires the thigh (knee) pad to be raised to its highest position. The user should sit erect on the seat and place the balls of the feet (metatarsals) on the nearest edge of the step with the legs and feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other.
summary: Starting position for the Seated Calf (Heel) Raise (Machine) exercise.

content: The starting position for the Seated Calf (Heel) Raise (Machine) exercise requires the thigh (knee) pad to be raised to its highest position. The user should sit erect on the seat and place the balls of the feet (metatarsals) on the nearest edge of the step with the legs and feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other. If the seat height is adjustable, it should be positioned to place the thighs parallel to the floor when the feet are in position. The thigh (knee) pad should be lowered so it firmly presses against the front of the lower thigh area. The user should grasp the handles.
summary: Describes the starting position for the Seated Calf Raise machine exercise.

content: All repetitions begin from this starting position. The upward movement of the Seated Calf (Heel) Raise (Machine) exercise begins with slowly plantar flexing the ankles under control. The torso should be kept erect and the legs and feet parallel to each other. Even pressure should be applied on all of the metatarsals; the feet should not be slightly inverted or everted to rise up only on the big or little toes. The arms should not be used to pull on the handles or thigh (knee) pad to help raise the weight. The upward movement should continue until the calf muscles are fully contracted (i.e., the ankles are fully plantar flexed).
summary: Details the execution of the Seated Calf Raise exercise.

content: At the completion of the set, slightly plantar flex the ankles, return the support back into place, and then stand up and step out of the machine.
summary: Describes the end-of-set procedure for the Seated Calf Raise machine.

content: The starting position for the flat barbell bench press requires the lifter to lie supine on a flat bench. The lifter should achieve a five-point body-contact position. The head should be placed firmly on the bench. The shoulders and upper back should be placed firmly and evenly on the bench. The buttocks should be placed evenly on the bench. The right foot should be flat on the floor. The left foot should be flat on the floor.
summary: Outlines the starting position for the flat barbell bench press.

content: The head should be placed firmly on the bench. The shoulders and upper back should be placed firmly and evenly on the bench. The buttocks should be placed evenly on the bench. The right foot should be flat on the floor. The left foot should be flat on the floor. The body should be adjusted on the bench to position the eyes directly below the racked bar.
summary: Proper bench press setup involves positioning the head, shoulders, back, and feet securely on the bench.

content: The lifter should grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. The spotter should stand erect behind the head of the bench. The spotter should place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. The spotter should grasp the bar with a closed, alternated grip inside the lifter’s hands.
summary: Describes the grip and stance for both the lifter and spotter during a bench press.

content: The lifter should lower the bar slowly and under control toward the chest. The elbows should be moved down past the torso and slightly away from the body. The wrists should be kept stiff and the forearms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. The bar should be lowered to lightly touch the chest at approximately nipple level. The head, torso, hips, and feet should be kept in a five-point body-contact position. The spotter should keep the hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. The spotter should slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar.
summary: Details the descent phase of the bench press, emphasizing controlled movement and proper form for both lifter and spotter.

content: The lifter should press the bar up and slightly backward. The lifter should maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position. The lifter should keep the wrists stiff and the forearms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. The bar should be pressed up until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. At the completion of the set, the lifter should signal the spotter for assistance to rack the bar. The lifter should keep a grip on the bar until both ends are secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge.
summary: Describes the upward movement of the bench press, maintaining form and ensuring a safe return to the rack.

content: The spotter should keep the hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. The spotter should slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar. After the set is completed, the spotter should grasp the bar with an alternated grip inside the lifter’s hands. The spotter should guide the bar back onto the rack. The spotter should keep a grip on the bar until it is secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge.
summary: Details the spotter's actions during the ascent and after the set, ensuring safety and proper bar handling.

content: The thighs should be approximately parallel to the floor with the feet flat when adjusting the incline bench seat height. The head should be lower than the racked bar and resting at the top of the bench when adjusting the incline bench seat height. The bar should be able to be lifted off and returned to the supporting pins or ledge without hitting the top of the head or requiring the use of the legs to help reach the rack.
summary: Provides instructions for adjusting the incline bench seat height for a safe and comfortable bench press.

content: To begin the incline barbell bench press, sit on the seat of an incline bench and lean back to place the body in a five-point body-contact position. The head should be placed firmly against the bench. The shoulders and upper back should be placed firmly and evenly against the bench. The buttocks should be placed evenly on the seat. The right foot should be flat on the floor. The left foot should be flat on the floor. Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Signal the spotter for assistance in moving the bar off the rack to a position over the neck and face with the elbows fully extended. All repetitions begin from the liftoff position.
summary: Outlines the starting procedure for the incline barbell bench press, emphasizing proper form and safety.

content: The right foot should be flat on the floor. The left foot should be flat on the floor. The lifter positions the dumbbells against the front of the thighs with the dumbbell handles parallel to each other. The lifter sits on one end of a flat bench with the dumbbells resting on the thighs. The lifter lies back so the head rests on the other end of the bench. Once in the supine position, the lifter moves the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and signals the spotter for assistance in moving them to a position above the chest with the elbows extended and the forearms parallel to each other. The lifter then repositions the head, shoulders, buttocks, and feet to achieve a five-point body-contact position. The five-point body-contact position consists of the head placed firmly on the bench, the shoulders and upper back placed firmly and evenly on the bench, the buttocks placed evenly on the bench, the right foot flat on the floor, and the left foot flat on the floor.
summary: Describes the setup and initial positioning for the flat dumbbell bench press.

content: Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. The spotter should stand erect behind the head of the bench. The spotter should place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. The spotter should grasp the bar with a closed, alternated grip inside the lifter’s hands. At the lifter’s signal, the spotter should assist with moving the bar off the rack. The spotter should guide the bar to a position over the lifter’s neck and face. The spotter should release the bar smoothly.
summary: Details the role and positioning of the spotter during the barbell bench press.

content: Begin the exercise by lowering the bar slowly and under control. The bar will have a tendency to move away from the body, depending on the angle of the bench, but guide it toward the upper chest area. Move the elbows down past the torso and slightly away from the body. Keep the wrists stiff and the forearms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. The width of the grip will determine how parallel the forearms are to each other. Lower the bar to lightly touch the chest at approximately the upper one-third of the chest, between the clavicles and the nipples. Do not bounce the bar on the chest or arch the low back to raise the chest to meet the bar. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. Keep the hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. Slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar.
summary: Outlines the eccentric (lowering) phase of the barbell bench press.

content: Press the bar upward and slightly backward. To keep it from falling forward (because of the angled torso position), press the bar up so it passes close by the face as opposed to out away from the chest. Do not arch the low back, raise the hips, or push up with the legs (by trying to stand up); the body and feet should not move from their initial positions. Keep the wrists stiff and the forearms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. Continue pressing the bar up until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. At the completion of the set, signal the spotter for assistance to rack the bar, but keep a grip on the bar until both ends are secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge. Keep the hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. Slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the bar. At the lifter’s signal after the set is completed, grasp the bar with an alternated grip inside the lifter’s hands. Guide the bar back onto the rack. Keep a grip on the bar until it is secure and motionless on the supporting pins or ledge.
summary: Describes the concentric (lifting) phase of the barbell bench press and the finalization of the set.

content: The flat dumbbell bench press starts with the lifter grasping two dumbbells of equal weight with a closed grip. The most common dumbbell position is with the dumbbell handles in line with each other with the palms facing toward the feet.
summary: Describes the initial grip and positioning for the flat dumbbell bench press.

content: The lifter lies back so the head rests on the other end of the bench. Once in the supine position, the lifter moves the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and signals the spotter for assistance in moving them to a position above the chest with the elbows extended and the forearms parallel to each other. The lifter then repositions the head, shoulders, buttocks, and feet to achieve a five-point body-contact position. The five-point body-contact position consists of the head placed firmly on the bench, the shoulders and upper back placed firmly and evenly on the bench, the buttocks placed evenly on the bench, the right foot flat on the floor, and the left foot flat on the floor.
summary: Describes the initial setup for the dumbbell bench press, including positioning the lifter on the bench and the dumbbells above the chest.

content: The most common dumbbell position is with the dumbbell handles in line with each other with the palms facing toward the feet. Another option is to hold the dumbbells in a neutral position (i.e., parallel to each other with the palms facing each other.) All repetitions begin from this extended-elbow position with the dumbbells supported over the chest.
summary: Explains the two common grip options for dumbbell bench press.

content: The spotter gets into a low (but still erect) body position close to the head of the bench. To create a stable spotting position, the spotter gets into a fully lunged position with one knee on the floor and the foot of the other leg ahead of the rear knee and flat on the floor. The spotter grasps the lifter’s forearms near the wrists.
summary: Details the spotter's position and grip for the dumbbell bench press.

content: At the lifter’s signal, the spotter assists with moving the dumbbells to a position over the lifter’s chest. The spotter releases the lifter’s forearms smoothly.
summary: Describes the spotter's role in assisting the lifter at the start of the exercise.

content: The lifter begins the exercise by lowering the dumbbells slowly and under control toward the chest. To maintain a stable body position on the bench, the lifter lowers the dumbbells at the same rate. The lifter keeps the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other (unless using a neutral grip). The lifter minimizes all forward-to-backward and side-to-side movement.
summary: Outlines the lifter's technique for lowering the dumbbells during the eccentric phase.

content: The lifter guides the dumbbells down and slightly out to the lateral side of the chest, near the armpits and in the same vertical plane as the nipples. Usually, the lowest position of the dumbbells is a depth similar to that used in the flat barbell bench press. Lifters performing this exercise with the dumbbells in a neutral position can lower them farther, if desired, because the torso does not obstruct the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Specifies the path and depth of the dumbbell descent during the exercise.

content: The lifter does not arch the low back to raise the chest. The lifter keeps the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. The spotter keeps the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells descend. The spotter slightly flexes the torso (but keeps the back neutral) when following the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Emphasizes maintaining proper form and the spotter's role during the eccentric phase.

content: The lifter presses the dumbbells upward at the same rate and slightly toward each other to keep them under control. The lifter maintains the same stationary five-point body-contact position; does not lift the head or arch the low back, and does not lift the buttocks or feet. The lifter keeps the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other; does not allow the dumbbells to sway as they are pressed.
summary: Describes the lifter's technique for pressing the dumbbells upward during the concentric phase.

content: The lifter continues pressing the dumbbells up until the elbows are fully extended. The lifter keeps the forearms nearly parallel to each other; the dumbbells can move toward each other over the chest, but do not clang them together. At the completion of the set, the lifter first slowly lowers the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and then, one at a time, returns the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Completes the description of the dumbbell bench press movement and the end of a set.

content: Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells ascend. Slightly extend the torso (but keep the back neutral) when following the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Provides additional guidance for the spotter during the concentric phase.

content: Before picking up the dumbbells, check the seat of the incline bench. If the incline bench is adjustable, move the seat to allow for the following conditions: The thighs are approximately parallel to the floor (with the feet flat). The body is low enough so that the head is at the top of the bench. The dumbbells will not hit the uprights of any of the racks (if they are present) during the exercise.
summary: Describes the setup for the incline dumbbell bench press, including adjusting the bench and ensuring safety.

content: Grasp two dumbbells of equal weight with a closed grip. Position the outside surface of the bottom half of the dumbbells against the front of the thighs (the dumbbell handles are parallel to each other). Sit on the seat of an incline bench with the dumbbells resting on the thighs. Lean back to place the head at the top of the bench.
summary: Details the initial setup for the incline dumbbell bench press, including grip, dumbbell placement, and body positioning.

content: The dumbbells will not hit the uprights of any of the racks (if they are present) during the exercise. Grasp two dumbbells of equal weight with a closed grip. Position the outside surface of the bottom half of the dumbbells against the front of the thighs (the dumbbell handles are parallel to each other). Sit on the seat of an incline bench with the dumbbells resting on the thighs. Lean back to place the head at the top of the bench.
summary: Starting position for the incline dumbbell bench press: grip, positioning, and setup.

content: Once the inclined position is achieved, first move the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and then signal the spotter for assistance in moving them to a position above the neck and face with the elbows extended and arms parallel to each other. Reposition the head, shoulders, buttocks, and feet to achieve a five-point body-contact position: 1. The head is placed firmly against the bench. 2. The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly against the bench. 3. The buttocks are placed evenly on the seat. 4. The right foot is flat on the floor. 5. The left foot is flat on the floor.
summary: Initial dumbbell placement and achieving a stable five-point body contact.

content: The most common dumbbell position is with the dumbbell handles in line with each other with the palms facing toward the feet. Another option is to hold the dumbbells in a neutral position (i.e., parallel to each other with the palms facing each other). All repetitions begin from this extended-elbow position with the dumbbells supported over the neck and face.
summary: Common dumbbell grip variations for the incline dumbbell bench press.

content: Stand erect behind the head of the bench. Place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. Grasp the lifter’s forearms near the wrists. At the lifter’s signal, assist with moving the dumbbells to a position over the lifter’s neck and face. Release the lifter’s forearms smoothly.
summary: Spotter positioning and assisting the lifter to the starting position.

content: Begin the exercise by lowering the dumbbells slowly and under control toward the chest. Keep the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other (unless using a neutral grip). Minimize all forward-to-backward and side-to-side movement. Guide the dumbbells down and slightly out to the lateral side of the chest, near the armpits and in line with the upper one-third of the chest (between the clavicles and the nipples). Usually, the lowest position of the dumbbells is a depth similar to that used in the incline barbell bench press. Visualize a bar passing through both dumbbell handles: The lowest position of the dumbbells is where the imaginary bar would touch the upper one-third of the chest. Lifters performing this exercise with the dumbbells in a neutral position can lower them farther, if desired, because the torso does not obstruct the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Lowering phase of the incline dumbbell bench press: technique and depth.

content: Do not arch the low back to raise the chest. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells descend. Slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Maintaining proper form and body position during the lowering phase.

content: Press the dumbbells upward at the same rate and slightly toward each other to keep them under control. To keep them from falling forward (because of the angled torso position), press the dumbbells up over the shoulders (initially) and face (eventually) instead of out and away from the chest. Do not arch the low back, raise the hips, or push up with the legs (by trying to stand up). The body and feet should not move from their initial positions. Keep the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other. Do not allow the dumbbells to sway as they are pressed.
summary: Pressing phase of the incline dumbbell bench press: technique and control.

content: Continue pressing the dumbbells up until the elbows are fully extended. Keep the forearms nearly parallel to each other. The dumbbells can move toward each other over the chest, but do not clang them together. At the completion of the set, first slowly lower the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and then to the thighs; then, one at a time, return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Finalizing the repetition and returning to the starting position.

content: Do not allow the dumbbells to sway as they are pressed. Keep the forearms nearly parallel to each other. The dumbbells can move toward each other over the chest, but do not clang them together. Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells ascend.  At the completion of the set, first slowly lower the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and then to the thighs; then, one at a time, return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Instructions for performing dumbbell presses, emphasizing controlled movement and proper hand positioning.

content: Slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the dumbbells. The thighs are approximately parallel to the floor (with the feet flat). The body is in line with the handgrips (an imaginary line connecting both handgrips should cross the front of the chest at nipple height). The arms are positioned approximately parallel to the floor when the elbows are extended and.
summary: Body positioning guidelines for dumbbell presses, ensuring proper alignment and stability.

content: The head is placed firmly against the vertical back pad. The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly against the vertical back pad. The buttocks are placed evenly on the seat. The right foot is flat on the floor. The left foot is flat on the floor.
summary: Five-point body contact positions for a stable base during dumbbell presses.

content: Grasp the handles with a closed, pronated (or neutral, if desired) grip. If the machine has a foot pedal, use one foot to depress the pedal to move the handles forward. Grasp the handles. Slowly release the foot pedal and place the foot on the floor. If there is no foot pedal, grasp the handles one at a time and reposition the body in a five-point body-contact position. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting positions and grip instructions for a machine-based chest press exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by pushing the handles forward. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position; do not arch the low back, lift the buttocks or feet, lift the head off of the pad, or contract the abdominals (to flex the torso forward). Keep the wrists stiff and continue pushing the handles until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. Allow the handles to move toward the body slowly and under control. Keep the wrists stiff; the arms will be approximately parallel to the floor if the seat was adjusted properly before beginning the exercise. Guide the handles back to the chest; do not allow the handles to move backward rapidly to add a bounce to help with the next repetition. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position.
summary: Execution instructions for a machine-based chest press, emphasizing controlled movement and maintaining proper form.

content: At the completion of the set, reverse the foot pedal procedure or guide the handles backward to their resting position by releasing the grip on one handle at a time.
summary: Ending the set for a machine-based chest press exercise.

content: Sit on the seat and place the body in a five-point body-contact position. The head is placed firmly against the vertical back pad (if the back pad is long enough). The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly against the vertical back pad. The buttocks are placed evenly on the seat. The right foot is flat on the floor. The left foot is flat on the floor. Grasp the handles one at a time with a closed, neutral grip with the elbows slightly flexed. Position the upper arms, elbows, and forearms parallel to the floor with the hands even with or slightly ahead of the front surface of the shoulders or chest. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by moving the handles toward each other at the same rate in a wide arc. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position; do not arch the low back, lift the buttocks or feet, flex the torso forward, or jerk the torso or head forward.
summary: Starting position and execution instructions for a machine-based chest press with a wide arc.

content: Grasp the handles one at a time with a closed, neutral grip with the elbows slightly flexed. Position the upper arms, elbows, and forearms parallel to the floor with the hands even with or slightly ahead of the front surface of the shoulders or chest. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by moving the handles toward each other at the same rate in a wide arc. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position; do not arch the low back, lift the buttocks or feet, flex the torso forward, or jerk the torso or head forward. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms, elbows, and forearms parallel to the floor. Continue moving the handles toward each other until they meet in front of the face (or as far as they will move forward). Allow the handles to swing out and back slowly and under control. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms, elbows, and forearms parallel to the floor. Guide the handles back to be in line with the chest and each other; do not rapidly swing the handles back to add momentum to help with the next repetition. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. At the completion of the set, guide the handles backward by slightly twisting the body to each side to return each handle, one at a time, to its resting position.
summary: Instructions for performing a dumbbell chest press exercise, emphasizing proper form and body positioning.

content: Grasp two dumbbells of equal weight with a closed grip. Position the outside surface of the bottom half of the dumbbells against the front of the thighs (the dumbbell handles will be parallel to each other). Sit on one end of a flat bench with the dumbbells resting on the thighs. Lie back so the head rests on the other end of the bench. Once the supine position is achieved, first move the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and then signal the spotter for assistance in moving them to a position above the chest with the elbows extended and forearms parallel to each other. The head is placed firmly on the bench. The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly on the bench. The buttocks are placed evenly on the bench. The right foot is flat on the floor. The left foot is flat on the floor. The dumbbells should be in a neutral hand position with the handles parallel to each other and the elbows pointing out to the sides. Slightly flex the elbows and hold this flexed position throughout the exercise. All repetitions begin with the arms in this position with the dumbbells supported over the chest.
summary: Setting up for a dumbbell chest press exercise, including dumbbell grip, bench position, and initial arm placement.

content: Get into a low (but still erect) body position close to the head of the bench. To create a stable spotting position, get into a fully lunged position with one knee on the floor and the foot of the other leg ahead of the rear knee and flat on the floor. Grasp the lifter’s forearms near the wrists. At the lifter’s signal, assist with moving the dumbbells to a position over the lifter’s chest. Release the lifter’s forearms smoothly.
summary: Instructions for a spotter during a dumbbell chest press exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by lowering the dumbbells slowly in wide arcs under control. No movement should occur at the elbow joints; movement should occur only at the shoulders. To maintain a stable body position on the bench, lower the dumbbells at the same rate. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position with the dumbbell handles parallel to each other throughout the movement. The hands, wrists, forearms, elbows, upper arms, and shoulders should stay nearly in the same vertical plane. The elbows should transition from pointing out to the sides to pointing toward the floor during the downward movement. Continue to lower the dumbbells in wide arcs until they are level with the top of the chest. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells descend. Slightly flex the torso (but keep the back neutral) when following the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Instructions for the lowering phase of a dumbbell chest press exercise, emphasizing controlled movement and proper form.

content: The elbows should transition from pointing out to the sides to pointing toward the floor during the downward movement. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells descend. Slightly flex the torso (but keep the back neutral) when following the path of the dumbbells. Continue to lower the dumbbells in wide arcs until they are level with the top of the chest.
summary: This describes the eccentric phase of a dumbbell chest fly exercise, emphasizing proper form and body positioning.

content: Raise the dumbbells upward in wide arcs under control; imagine hugging a large tree trunk with the arms. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position; do not arch the low back, lift the buttocks or feet, lift the head, or shrug the shoulders to help raise the dumbbells. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position. The hands, wrists, forearms, elbows, upper arms, and shoulders should stay in nearly the same vertical plane during the upward movement. Continue raising the dumbbells until they are positioned over the chest in the starting position.
summary: This describes the concentric phase of a dumbbell chest fly exercise, emphasizing proper form and body positioning.

content: At the completion of the set, first slowly lower the dumbbells to the chest (armpit) area and then, one at a time, return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: This describes the final stage of a dumbbell chest fly exercise, emphasizing controlled lowering of the weights.

content: Before performing this exercise, check that the same weight is selected for both weight stacks. Stand near a weight stack (with the weight stacks behind the body) and reach back with the left (or right) hand to grasp the handle with a closed, neutral grip. Pull the handle down and toward the left (or right) side of the body. Move toward the other weight stack and grasp the other handle with the right (or left) hand with a closed, neutral grip. Pull the handle down and toward the right (or left) side of the body. Move to a position on the floor between the two weight stacks.
summary: This describes the setup and initial movement of a cable crossover exercise.

content: Slightly flex the elbows, take a step forward with one foot (to get into a staggered stance with the lead knee moderately flexed and the trailing knee fully extended) while allowing the shoulders to horizontally abduct (or diagonally abduct, depending on the height of the machine relative to the shoulders) away from and behind the torso. The cables should be taut in this position. Keeping the head in line with the spine, slightly flex the torso and check that the feet are pointing straight ahead. Check that the elbows are slightly flexed and the handles are held with a neutral grip; these positions are held throughout the exercise. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: This describes the starting position and body alignment for a cable crossover exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by adducting the shoulders in wide arcs under control. No movement should occur at the elbow joints; movement should occur only at the shoulders. To maintain a stable body position, move the handles toward each other at the same rate. Maintain the same slightly forward-flexed torso position. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position with the handles nearly parallel to each other throughout the movement. Continue to adduct the shoulders until the handles are brought together in front of the chest.
summary: This describes the execution of the cable crossover exercise, emphasizing controlled shoulder movement and maintaining proper form.

content: Allow the handles to swing out slowly and under control back to the starting position. Maintain the same slightly forward-flexed torso position. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position with the handles nearly parallel to each other. At the completion of the set, take a step back and toward one of the weight stacks and guide the handle toward the machine to slowly return the weight stack to a resting position. Then, take a step toward the other weight stack and guide the handle toward the machine to slowly return the remaining weight stack to a resting position.
summary: This describes the return movement and finalization of the cable crossover exercise.

content: The long bar used in the lat pulldown exercise can be 36 to 48 inches [91 to 122 cm] long with slightly angled ends. A common grip width for the long bar involves placing the index finger on the outside bends of the bar. If the bar is entirely straight, use a grip wider than shoulder-width and spaced evenly on the bar.
summary: This describes the specifications and grip options for the long bar used in a lat pulldown exercise.

content: If a seat is attached to the lat pulldown machine, sit down facing the weight stack with the legs under the thigh pads and the feet flat on the floor. If the bench seat is adjustable, position the thighs approximately parallel to the floor with the feet flat on the floor.
summary: This describes the proper seating position for a lat pulldown exercise.

content: The long bar used in the lat pulldown exercise can be 36 to 48 inches [91 to 122 cm] long with slightly angled ends. A common grip width for the long bar involves placing the index finger on the outside bends of the bar. If the bar is entirely straight, use a grip wider than shoulder-width and spaced evenly on the bar.
summary: Describes the dimensions and grip options for the long bar used in the lat pulldown exercise.

content: If there is no seat attached to the machine, kneel on one knee facing the machine and under the top pulley. The foot of the other leg is placed ahead of the body, flat on the floor. If a seat is attached to the lat pulldown machine, sit down facing the weight stack with the legs under the thigh pads and the feet flat on the floor. If the bench seat is adjustable, position the thighs approximately parallel to the floor with the feet flat on the floor.
summary: Provides instructions for positioning the body on a lat pulldown machine, whether with or without a seat.

content: Before beginning the lat pulldown exercise, lean the torso slightly backward and extend the neck to create a clear path for the bar to pass by the face as it is pulled down. This position will also reduce impingement stress on the shoulder joints. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Outlines the proper starting position for the lat pulldown exercise, emphasizing torso angle and neck extension.

content: Begin the lat pulldown exercise by pulling down on the bar; the elbows should move down and back and the chest up and out as the bar is lowered. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not jerk the torso or lean back quickly to help pull the bar down. Continue pulling the bar down and toward the body (not just down) until it lightly touches the clavicle and upper chest area. The torso should still have a slight backward lean at the bottom bar position.
summary: Describes the movement pattern for the lat pulldown exercise, emphasizing controlled movement and proper form.

content: Guide the bar slowly and under control back up to the starting position; do not allow the bar to jerk the arms upward. Maintain the same backward torso lean and lower body position. The elbows should be fully extended at the end of the upward movement. At the completion of the set, stand up slowly and guide the bar under control to its resting position.
summary: Details the eccentric phase of the lat pulldown exercise, emphasizing controlled movement and maintaining proper form.

content: Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, wider than shoulder-width apart. Follow the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to lift the bar off the floor to a position at the front of the thighs. The body should be fully erect before moving into the flexed torso position of the bent-over row.
summary: Provides instructions for the starting position and grip for the bent-over row exercise.

content: Place the feet shoulder-width apart (or slightly wider) with the knees slightly flexed. Flex forward at the hips so the torso is nearly parallel to the floor while maintaining the same flexed knee position. Pull the shoulders back, push the chest out, and extend the neck slightly to create a neutral or slightly concave (not rounded) back position. Do not attempt to look up at the ceiling; just focus on the floor a short distance ahead of the feet.
summary: Describes the body positioning for the bent-over row exercise, emphasizing a neutral spine and proper form.
