content: Allow the bar to hang at full elbow extension; adjust the amount of knee and torso flexion so the weight plates are not touching the floor. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by pulling the bar up toward the torso; the elbows should point out to the sides, away from the body, with the wrists kept straight. Do not curl the bar upward.
summary: Details the starting position and initial movement for the bent-over row exercise.

content: Maintain the same stationary body position; do not shrug the shoulders, swing the body (i.e., extend the spine), hyperextend the neck, extend the knees, or rise up on the toes to help raise the bar upward. Continue pulling the bar up until it touches the sternum or upper abdomen. At the highest bar position, the elbows will be higher than the torso (when seen from the side).
summary: Emphasizes maintaining proper form during the bent-over row exercise, avoiding common mistakes.

content: Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position; do not flex the torso forward, extend the knees, or allow the body’s weight to shift toward the toes. Maintain the same stationary torso, neutral spine, and flexed knee positions with the feet flat on the floor. The elbows should be fully extended at the end of the downward movement. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate to squat down and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Describes the eccentric phase of the bent-over row exercise, emphasizing controlled movement and maintaining proper form.

content: Stand on the side of a flat bench (with the body perpendicular, not parallel, to the length of the bench) with a dumbbell on the floor. Position the feet shoulder-width apart and slightly flex the knees. Reach down with one hand and grasp the dumbbell with a closed, neutral grip.
summary: Provides instructions for the starting position and grip for the dumbbell row exercise.

content: Stand on the side of a flat bench (with the body perpendicular, not parallel, to the length of the bench) with a dumbbell on the floor. Position the feet shoulder-width apart and slightly flex the knees. Reach down with one hand and grasp the dumbbell with a closed, neutral grip. Flex forward at the hips so the torso is slightly above parallel to the floor with the dumbbell hanging down at full elbow extension. The opposite hand should be placed on the bench for support. Pull the shoulders back, push the chest out, and slightly extend the neck to create a neutral spine position. Do not attempt to look up at the ceiling; just focus on the floor a short distance ahead of the feet. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for a dumbbell row exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by pulling the dumbbell up toward the torso; the upper arm and elbow should be kept near the side of the body with the wrist straight. Do not curl the dumbbell upward or inward. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not swing or jerk the upper body upward to help raise the dumbbell. Continue pulling the dumbbell up until it touches the side of the outer chest or rib cage area. At the highest dumbbell position, the elbow will be higher than the torso (when seen from the side). Lower the dumbbell slowly and under control to the starting position; do not allow the dumbbell to jerk the arm down. Maintain the same stationary torso, neutral spine, and flexed knee positions with both feet flat on the floor. After completing a set with one arm, repeat the procedure using the other arm.
summary: Details the execution of a dumbbell row exercise.

content: The low-pulley seated row exercise begins with the user sitting on the long seat pad or the floor facing the machine. The user places their feet on the foot supports or the machine frame. The user flexes their knees and hips to reach forward and grasp the handles with a closed, neutral grip. The user pulls the handles back to sit in an erect seated position with the torso perpendicular to the floor, knees slightly flexed, and the feet and legs parallel to each other. The elbows should be fully extended and the arms parallel to the floor (or slightly below) with the selected load suspended above the remainder of the weight stack. All repetitions begin from this starting position.
summary: Describes the setup for a seated row exercise.

content: The exercise begins with pulling the handles toward the abdomen. The elbows should stay relatively near or next to the sides of the torso, not pointing directly out to the sides. The user maintains the same stationary body position and does not jerk the torso, extend the knees, or quickly lean back to help pull the handles. The user continues pulling the handles until the forearms or wrists press against the torso, or until the handles touch the abdomen. The user guides the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position. The user maintains the same stationary torso and flexed knee positions. The elbows should be fully extended at the end of the forward movement. At the completion of the set, the user slowly flexes the knees and hips to move forward and return the handles to the resting position.
summary: Details the execution of a seated row exercise.

content: Before performing the seated row exercise, the user checks the height of the seat and position of the chest pad and adjusts them to allow for the following conditions: the thighs are approximately parallel to the floor (with the feet flat or on the foot supports), the torso is perpendicular to the floor when sitting erect with the torso against the chest pad, and the arms are approximately parallel to the floor when holding the handles. The user sits erect with the feet flat on the floor or on the foot supports and presses the torso against the chest pad. The user grasps the handles with a closed, pronated grip (or neutral grip, if desired) and then repositions the body to achieve the seated erect torso position. The elbows should be fully extended and the arms approximately parallel to the floor with the selected load suspended above the remainder of the weight stack. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by pulling the handles toward the torso, chest, upper abdomen, or lower abdomen, depending on the type of machine. The elbows should stay relatively near or next to the sides of the torso (depending on what handle is used), not pointing directly out to the sides. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not lean back quickly to help pull the handles. Continue pulling the handles until the forearms or wrists press against the torso or until the handles touch the torso.
summary: Provides detailed instructions for adjusting and performing a seated row exercise.

content: The elbows should be fully extended at the end of the downward movement. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate to squat down and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Describes the end of a squat set.

content: Begin the exercise by pulling the handles toward the torso, chest, upper abdomen, or lower abdomen, depending on the type of machine. The elbows should stay relatively near or next to the sides of the torso (depending on what handle is used), not pointing directly out to the sides. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not lean back quickly to help pull the handles. Continue pulling the handles until the forearms or wrists press against the torso or until the handles touch the torso. Allow the handles to move slowly and under control to the starting position; do not allow the handles to jerk the arms forward.
summary: This describes the pulling phase of a cable exercise, emphasizing controlled movement and proper elbow position.

content: Stand facing a high-pulley cable machine and grasp a rope handle with a closed, pronated grip and the palms facing the floor. Step back far enough from the machine so there is tension on the cable when the arms are fully extended in front of the face with the elbows pointing out to the sides. Use a parallel foot stance with flexed knees to create a stable body position. Check that the head is in line with the spine and the torso is fully erect. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: This outlines the setup and starting position for a cable exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by first retracting the scapulae with the elbows still fully extended. Pull the handle toward the face by horizontally abducting the shoulders and flexing the elbows. When the upper arms become aligned with the frontal plane at the shoulders, externally rotate the upper arms and continue the backward movement until the center clasp of the handle comes close to the face. Allow the upper arms to internally rotate, the elbows to extend, the shoulders to horizontally adduct, and the scapulae to protract back to the starting position. Maintain the same head, torso, and body position throughout the movement.
summary: This describes the movement pattern for a cable row exercise.

content: Before performing the shoulder press, adjust the seat height so that the thighs are parallel to the floor with the feet flat. The shoulders should be approximately in line with the handgrips, with an imaginary line connecting both handgrips not crossing lower than the top of the shoulders or the base of the neck. The body should be low enough that the head is in contact with the pad at the top of the bench. In the starting position, the head is placed firmly against the vertical back pad. The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly against the vertical back pad. The buttocks are placed evenly on the seat. The right foot is flat on the floor. The left foot is flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with a closed, pronated grip or a neutral grip. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: This details the setup and starting position for a seated shoulder press.

content: Begin the exercise by pressing the handles upward. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position during the upward movement. Do not arch the low back, raise the buttocks, or push upward with the legs during the upward movement. Keep the wrists stiff and the forearms approximately parallel to each other during the upward movement. Continue pressing the handles until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. Allow the handles to move slowly and under control back to the starting position. Do not flex the torso forward as the handles are lowered. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position during the downward movement. Do not allow the handles to move downward rapidly to add a bounce to help with the next repetition. At the completion of the set, return the handles to the resting position.
summary: This describes the movement pattern for a seated shoulder press, emphasizing controlled movement and proper form.

content: Before performing the seated barbell shoulder press, check the seat of the shoulder press bench. If the seat is adjustable, move it to allow for the thighs to be approximately parallel to the floor with the feet flat. The head should be lower than the racked bar and resting at the top of the bench if the bench has a long vertical back pad. The thighs should be approximately parallel to the floor with the feet flat when adjusting the seat of the bench. The body should be low enough so that the head is at the top of the bench if the bench has a long vertical back pad.
summary: Proper setup for the seated barbell shoulder press involves adjusting the bench seat and body position for optimal form.

content: Grasp two equal-weight dumbbells with a closed grip. Position the outside surface of the bottom half of the dumbbells against the front of the thighs with the dumbbell handles parallel to each other. Handles are level with the top of the shoulders or the base of the neck. The most common dumbbell position is with the dumbbell handles in line with each other with the palms facing away from the face. Another option is to hold the dumbbells in a neutral position (i.e., parallel to each other with the palms facing each other). All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for dumbbell shoulder press involves grip, dumbbell placement, and common variations.

content: Stand erect behind the back of the bench. Place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. Grasp the lifter’s forearms near the wrists. At the lifter’s signal, assist with moving the dumbbells to a position outside the shoulders. Release the lifter’s forearms smoothly.
summary: Assistance during dumbbell shoulder press setup involves positioning and initial lift.

content: Begin the exercise by pressing the dumbbells upward at the same rate and slightly toward each other to keep them under control. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position; do not arch the low back, raise the buttocks, or push upward with the legs. Keep the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other; do not allow the dumbbells to sway as they are pressed. The hands, elbows, and shoulders should be in the same vertical plane. Continue pressing the dumbbells up until the elbows are fully extended. Keep the forearms nearly parallel to each other; the dumbbells can move toward each other over the head, but do not clang them together. Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells ascend. Slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the dumbbells.
summary: Executing the dumbbell shoulder press involves controlled movement, maintaining form, and avoiding common errors.

content: Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control to the starting position. To maintain a stable body position on the bench, lower the dumbbells at the same rate. Keep the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other. Continue to lower the dumbbells until they are level with the top of the shoulders or the base of the neck; do not bounce the dumbbells on the shoulders or shrug the shoulders to meet the dumbbells. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. At the completion of the set, return the dumbbells to the resting position. Allow the handles to move slowly and under control back to the starting position. Do not flex the torso forward as the handles are lowered. Do not allow the handles to move downward rapidly to add a bounce to help with the next repetition.
summary: The eccentric phase of the dumbbell shoulder press emphasizes controlled lowering, maintaining form, and avoiding momentum.

content: Grasp the bar evenly with a closed and pronated grip, approximately shoulder-width or slightly wider apart. Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed, torso erect, shoulders held back, and eyes focused ahead. Allow the bar to hang at full elbow extension. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by pulling the bar up along the abdomen and chest by abducting the shoulders and flexing the elbows. Keep the elbows pointed out to the sides as the bar brushes against the body; do not curl the bar upward. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not shrug the shoulders, swing the body (i.e., hyperextend the spine), hyperextend the neck, extend the knees, or rise up on the toes to help raise the bar upward. Continue pulling the bar up until it reaches the area between the bottom of the sternum and the chin (depending on arm length and shoulder flexibility). At the highest bar position, the elbows should be level with or slightly higher than the shoulders and wrists. Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position; do not flex the torso forward, bounce the bar on the thighs at the bottom position, or allow the body’s weight to shift toward the toes. Maintain the same stationary body position with the feet flat on the floor. The elbows should be fully extended at the end of the downward movement. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to squat down and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: This describes the proper form for a barbell row, emphasizing a stationary body position and controlled movements throughout the lift.

content: Grasp two dumbbells of equal weight with a closed, neutral grip. Follow the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to lift the dumbbells off the floor to a position hanging next to the thighs. Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed, torso erect, shoulders held back, and eyes focused ahead. Move the dumbbells to the front of the thighs and position them with the palms facing each other (this creates a neutral grip position). Slightly flex the elbows and hold this flexed position throughout the exercise. (Note: The elbows should be slightly more flexed than what is seen in the photos and the video clip.) All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the exercise by raising the dumbbells up and out to the sides. No movement should occur at the elbow joints; movement should occur only at the shoulders. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position; maintain a neutral grip on the dumbbells. The upper arms and elbows should rise together ahead of the forearms, hands, and dumbbells. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not shrug the shoulders, swing the body (i.e., hyperextend the spine), extend the knees, or rise up on the toes to help raise the dumbbells. Continue raising the dumbbells until the arms are parallel to the floor or approximately level with the shoulders. Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control to the starting position; do not flex the torso forward, extend the knees, or allow the body’s weight to shift toward the toes. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not allow the dumbbells to jerk the arms down. Continue to lower the dumbbells until they return to the front of the thighs; do not bounce the dumbbells on the thighs to help with the next repetition. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to squat down and return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: This describes the proper form for a dumbbell front raise, emphasizing a stationary body position and controlled movements throughout the lift.

content: The bent-over lateral raise starts with grasping two equal-weight dumbbells with a closed grip. The body should be fully erect before moving into the flexed torso position of the bent-over lateral raise. Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed.
summary: This describes the starting position for a bent-over lateral raise.

content: Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control to the starting position. To maintain a stable body position on the bench, lower the dumbbells at the same rate. Keep the wrists stiff, the forearms perpendicular to the floor, and the dumbbell handles in line with each other. Continue to lower the dumbbells until they are level with the top of the shoulders or the base of the neck; do not bounce the dumbbells on the shoulders or shrug the shoulders to meet the dumbbells. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position. At the completion of the set, slowly lower the dumbbells to the thighs; then, one at a time, return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner. Keep the hands near—but not touching—the lifter’s forearms near the wrists as the dumbbells descend. Slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso, and keep the back neutral when following the path of the dumbbells.
summary: This describes the proper form for lowering dumbbells during a dumbbell bench press, emphasizing controlled movements and maintaining a stable body position.

content: The bent-over lateral raise starts with grasping two equal-weight dumbbells with a closed grip. Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. Flex the torso forward to slightly above parallel to the floor while maintaining the same flexed knee position. Pull the shoulders back, push the chest out, and extend the neck slightly to create a neutral or slightly concave (not rounded) back position. Do not attempt to look up at the ceiling; just focus on the floor a short distance ahead of the feet. Allow the dumbbells to hang; adjust the amount of knee and torso flexion so the dumbbells are not touching the floor. Reposition the dumbbells in a neutral hand position with the handles parallel to each other and the elbows pointing out to the sides. Slightly flex the elbows and hold the flexed position throughout the exercise. The elbows should be slightly more flexed than what is seen in the photos and the video clip. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: The bent-over lateral raise begins with a closed grip on dumbbells, maintaining a neutral spine and slightly flexed knees.

content: Begin the exercise by raising the dumbbells up and out to the sides. No movement should occur at the elbow joints; movement should occur only at the shoulders. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position; maintain a neutral grip on the dumbbells. The upper arms, elbows, forearms, and dumbbells should stay nearly in the same vertical plane (perpendicular to the body) during the upward movement. The elbows should rise together and ahead of and slightly higher than the dumbbells. Maintain the neutral spine, stationary torso, and flexed knee positions with the feet flat on the floor; do not swing the body (i.e., extend the spine), extend the knees, or rise up on the toes to help raise the dumbbells. Continue raising the dumbbells until the upper arms are approximately parallel to the floor or approximately level with the shoulders. At the highest position, the elbows will be slightly higher than the dumbbells (when seen from the side).
summary: The upward movement of the bent-over lateral raise involves raising the dumbbells in a controlled manner, focusing on shoulder movement and maintaining a neutral spine.

content: Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control to the starting position; do not flex the torso forward, extend the knees, or allow the body’s weight to shift toward the toes. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows locked in a slightly flexed position. Maintain the neutral spine, stationary torso, and flexed knee positions with the feet flat on the floor. Continue to lower the dumbbells until they return to their hanging starting position; keep the dumbbell handles parallel to each other during the downward movement. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate to squat down and return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: The downward movement of the bent-over lateral raise involves controlled lowering of the dumbbells, maintaining a neutral spine and controlled movement.

content: The barbell biceps curl exercise commonly uses a cambered bar, also known as an EZ-curl bar. A cambered bar has two distinct hand placements: an inside and an outside grip. Holding the bar to form an M with the bends allows the hands to grasp the bar evenly with a narrow, inside grip. Reversing the bar to form a W with the bends results in a wide, outside grip. Either grip can be used for the barbell biceps curl.
summary: The barbell biceps curl can be performed with an EZ-curl bar, offering both inside and outside grip options.

content: In the starting position for the barbell biceps curl, grasp the bar evenly with a closed, supinated grip. The preparatory body position and lifting guidelines should be followed to lift the bar off the floor to a position at the front of the thighs. A common grip width for the barbell biceps curl involves placing the hands on the bar so the arms touch the sides of the torso or hips. The nonthumb sides of the hands should be next to (and in contact with) the sides of the thighs. Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed, torso erect, shoulders held back, and eyes focused ahead. The bar should rest against the front of the thighs at full elbow extension. All repetitions of the barbell biceps curl begin from this starting position.
summary: The starting position for the barbell biceps curl involves a supinated grip, proper body positioning, and the bar resting at the front of the thighs.

content: Begin the upward movement of the barbell biceps curl by raising the bar in an arc by flexing the elbows. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms stationary against the sides of the torso as the bar is raised.
summary: The upward movement of the barbell biceps curl involves flexing the elbows and keeping the upper arms stationary.

content: Continue to lower the dumbbells until they return to the front of the thighs; do not bounce the dumbbells on the thighs to help with the next repetition. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to squat down and return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: The barbell biceps curl involves lowering the dumbbells in a controlled manner and returning to the starting position.

content: Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed, torso erect, shoulders held back, and eyes focused ahead. The bar should rest against the front of the thighs at full elbow extension. All repetitions of the barbell biceps curl begin from this starting position.
summary: Describes the starting position for the barbell biceps curl.

content: Begin the upward movement of the barbell biceps curl by raising the bar in an arc by flexing the elbows. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms stationary against the sides of the torso as the bar is raised. Do not let the upper arms move forward or outward. No movement should occur at the shoulders; movement should occur only at the elbow joints. Maintain the same stationary body position during the upward movement; do not swing the body (i.e., hyperextend the spine), shrug the shoulders, hyperextend the neck, extend the knees, or rise up on the toes to help raise the bar upward. Continue flexing the elbows until the bar is near the anterior deltoids. If the elbows move forward at the highest bar position, then the elbows have flexed too far.
summary: Details the proper form for the upward phase of the barbell biceps curl.

content: Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position by extending the elbows during the downward movement. Do not bounce the bar on the thighs at the bottom position, flex the torso forward, extend the knees, or allow the body’s weight to shift toward the toes. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms stationary against the sides of the torso. Maintain the same stationary body position with the feet flat on the floor during the downward movement. Continue to lower the bar until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to squat down and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Describes the proper form for the downward phase of the barbell biceps curl.

content: The exercise begins with raising one dumbbell upward in an arc by flexing the elbow. The other arm should be kept stationary at the side of the thigh. The wrist should be kept stiff and the upper arm stationary against the side of the torso as the dumbbell is raised. Movement should occur only at the elbow joint. The dumbbell should remain in a neutral position as it is raised. Maintain the same stationary body position; do not swing the body, shrug the shoulders, hyperextend the neck, extend the knees, or rise up on the toes. Continue flexing the elbow until the top half of the dumbbell is near the anterior deltoid in a neutral hand position. If the elbow moves forward at the highest dumbbell position, then the elbow has flexed too far. Lower the dumbbell slowly and under control to the starting position, maintaining a neutral grip. Do not flex the torso forward, extend the knees, or allow the body’s weight to shift toward the toes. Keep the wrist stiff and the upper arm stationary against the side of the torso. Maintain the same stationary body position with the feet flat on the floor. Continue to lower the dumbbell until the elbow is fully extended but not forcefully locked. Repeat the upward and downward movements with the other arm; the arm just used should remain stationary until the dumbbell in the other hand returns to the starting position. Continue to alternate arms to complete the set. At the completion of the set, slowly flex the hips and knees at the same rate (to keep an erect torso position) to squat down and return the dumbbells to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Describes the proper form for the dumbbell biceps curl.

content: Sit on one end of a flat bench and then lie back so the head rests on the other end of the bench. Position the head, shoulders, buttocks, and feet to achieve a five-point body-contact position. The head is placed firmly on the bench. The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly on the bench. The buttocks are placed evenly on the bench. The right foot is flat on the floor. The left foot is flat on the floor. Signal the spotter to pick up the bar off the floor.
summary: Describes the starting position for a bench press.

content: The shoulders and upper back are placed firmly and evenly on the bench. The buttocks are placed evenly on the bench. Stand erect behind the head of the bench. Place the feet shoulder-width apart in a staggered stance with the knees slightly flexed.
summary: Describes the proper starting position for bench press.

content: Signal the spotter to pick up the bar off the floor. Grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. Move the bar to a position above the chest with the elbows extended and forearms parallel to each other. Externally rotate the arms slightly so the elbows point away from the face (toward the knees). All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Details the setup and starting position for bench press.

content: Begin the exercise by lowering the bar in an arc slowly and under control toward the nose, eyes, forehead, or the top of the head, depending on the length of the arms. Lifters with longer arms will lower the bar toward the top of the head and those with shorter arms will lower the bar toward the face. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. No movement should occur at the shoulder joints; movement should occur only at the elbows.
summary: Describes the eccentric phase of the bench press.

content: As the elbows begin to flex, they should point toward the knees (not out to the sides). Continue to lower the bar until it almost touches the head or face at its lowest position. Keep the head, torso, hips, and feet in a five-point body-contact position.
summary: Continues the description of the eccentric phase of the bench press.

content: Push the bar upward under control by extending at the elbows back to the starting position. No movement should occur at the shoulder joints; movement should occur only at the elbows. Keep the upper arms and elbows stationary; they should not move forward or out as the bar rises. Maintain the same stationary five-point body-contact position; do not arch the low back, lift the head, raise the buttocks, or push up with the legs.
summary: Describes the concentric phase of the bench press.

content: Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. Continue to push the bar up until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. At the completion of the set, signal the spotter to take the bar, but keep a firm grip until the spotter gains full control of the bar.
summary: Continues the description of the concentric phase of the bench press and the end of the set.

content: Stand under a high-pulley station with the torso erect, shoulders held back, back against the vertical back pad (if one is present), head in a neutral position, and eyes focused ahead. Grasp the bar evenly with a closed, pronated grip, approximately 6 to 12 inches (15 to 30 cm) wide. A minimum recommended grip width is close enough for the tips of the thumbs to touch each other when they are extended along the bar. A maximum distance is where the forearms are parallel to each other.
summary: Describes the starting position for a high pulley exercise.

content: Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect. Pull the bar down to position the upper arms and elbows against the sides of the torso with the forearms parallel to the floor (or slightly higher).
summary: Continues the description of the starting position and the movement for a high pulley exercise.

content: Hand the bar to the lifter. Guide the bar to a position over the lifter’s chest. Release the bar smoothly.
summary: Describes the actions of the spotter during a bench press.

content: At the lifter’s signal after the set is completed, stand up and grasp the bar with a closed, alternated grip, take it from the lifter, and set it on the floor.
summary: Describes the actions of the spotter at the end of a bench press set.

content: The right foot is flat on the floor. The left foot is flat on the floor.
summary: Describes the foot placement for a barbell exercise.

content: Grasp the bar evenly with a closed, pronated grip, approximately 6 to 12 inches (15 to 30 cm) wide. A minimum recommended grip width is close enough for the tips of the thumbs to touch each other when they are extended along the bar. A maximum distance is where the forearms are parallel to each other.
summary: Describes the proper grip width for a barbell exercise.

content: Place the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect.  The body should be close enough to the machine so the cable hangs nearly perpendicular to the floor when the bar is grasped and held in the starting position.
summary: Specifies the starting stance and body position for a cable exercise.

content: Pull the bar down to position the upper arms and elbows against the sides of the torso with the forearms parallel to the floor (or slightly higher). Do not lean forward or turn the head to position an ear next to the cable; instead, keep the head in a neutral position with the cable directly in front of the nose.
summary: Details the initial pulling motion and head position for a cable exercise.

content: Hold the shoulders back, keep the upper arms and elbows pressed against the sides of the torso, and keep the abdominal muscles contracted. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Outlines the proper form and starting position for a cable exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by pushing the bar down by extending at the elbows. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms perpendicular to the floor and pressed against the sides of the torso. No movement should occur at the shoulder joints; movement should occur only at the elbows.
summary: Describes the execution of the pushing phase of a cable exercise.

content: Continue to push the bar down until the elbows are fully extended but not forcefully locked. Maintain an erect torso and slightly flexed knee position; do not squat down slightly, lean forward, move the elbows backward, or move the cable to the right or left to help push the bar down.
summary: Provides further instructions on the pushing phase of a cable exercise.

content: Guide the bar slowly and under control back up to the starting position; do not allow the bar to jerk the arms upward. Keep the upper arms and elbows stationary; they should not move forward or out as the bar rises.
summary: Details the controlled lifting phase of a cable exercise.

content: Maintain the same stationary body position; do not move the head, torso, or feet as the bar rises. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms perpendicular to the floor and pressed against the sides of the torso. Continue to guide the bar up until the forearms are parallel to the floor (or slightly higher).
summary: Continues the description of the lifting phase of a cable exercise.

content: At the completion of the set, slowly guide the bar under control to its resting position.
summary: Completes the instructions for a cable exercise.

content: Grasp the bar evenly with a closed, supinated grip about hip- to shoulder-width apart. Follow the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to lift the bar off the floor to a position at the front of the thighs.
summary: Describes the starting grip and initial lift for a barbell exercise.

content: Sit on one end of a flat bench and position the feet hip-width apart with the legs parallel to each other and the toes pointing straight ahead. Lean the torso forward to place the elbows and forearms on top of the thighs.
summary: Details the starting position for a barbell exercise on a bench.

content: Move the forearms forward until the wrists extend slightly beyond the patellae. Open the hands to allow the wrists to extend in order to rest the back of the hands on the patellae, and then roll the bar down so it is held by the fingertips. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Continues the description of the starting position for a barbell exercise on a bench.

content: Begin the exercise by raising the bar by flexing the fingers and then the wrists. Keep the elbows and forearms stationary; do not jerk the shoulders backward or rise up on the toes to help raise the bar upward.
summary: Describes the execution of the lifting phase of a barbell exercise on a bench.

content: Continue flexing the wrists as far as possible without lifting the wrists off the thighs. Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position by extending the fingers and wrists; do not lift the elbows off the thighs.
summary: Continues the description of the lifting and lowering phases of a barbell exercise on a bench.

content: Maintain the same stationary body and arm positions with the feet flat on the floor. At the completion of the set, lift the arms off the thighs, slowly lean forward, and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Completes the instructions for a barbell exercise on a bench.

content: The wrist extension exercise starts with grasping the bar evenly with a closed, pronated grip about hip- to shoulder-width apart. The lifter should follow the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to lift the bar off the floor to a position at the front of the thighs. The lifter should sit on one end of a flat bench and position the feet hip-width apart with the legs parallel to each other and the toes pointing straight ahead.
summary: Describes the starting grip and initial lift for a wrist extension exercise.

content: The lifter should lean the torso forward to place the elbows and forearms on the top of the thighs. The lifter should move the wrists forward until they are slightly beyond the patellae. The lifter should keep a closed grip on the bar but allow the wrists to flex toward the floor.
summary: Continues the description of the starting position for a wrist extension exercise.

content: The lifter should follow the preparatory body position and lifting guidelines to lift the bar off the floor to a position at the front of the thighs. The lifter should sit on one end of a flat bench and position the feet hip-width apart with the legs parallel to each other and the toes pointing straight ahead. The lifter should lean the torso forward to place the elbows and forearms on the top of the thighs. The lifter should move the wrists forward until they are slightly beyond the patellae. The lifter should keep a closed grip on the bar but allow the wrists to flex toward the floor. At the completion of the set, the lifter should lift the arms off the thighs, slowly lean forward, and return the bar to the floor in a controlled manner.
summary: Instructions for performing a barbell exercise, emphasizing proper body positioning and bar handling.

content: All repetitions of the wrist extension exercise begin from the position with the wrists flexed toward the floor. The upward movement of the wrist extension exercise begins by raising the bar by extending the wrists. During the upward movement, the lifter should keep the elbows and forearms stationary and not jerk the shoulders backward or rise up on the toes to help raise the bar upward. The lifter should continue extending the wrists as far as possible without lifting the wrists off the thighs. The downward movement of the wrist extension exercise involves lowering the bar slowly and under control to the starting position. During the downward movement, the lifter should not lift the elbows off the thighs. The lifter should maintain the same stationary body and arm positions with the feet flat on the floor during the downward movement.
summary: Detailed steps for performing a wrist extension exercise, focusing on controlled movement and proper form.

content: The bent-knee sit-up exercise starts with lying in a supine position on a floor mat. The lifter should flex the knees to approximately 90 degrees and flex the hips to approximately 45 degrees to place the feet flat on the mat with the heels near the buttocks. The thighs, knees, and feet should be aligned with each other. The lifter should fold the arms across the chest or abdomen. All repetitions of the bent-knee sit-up exercise begin from the position with the knees flexed to approximately 90 degrees and hips flexed to approximately 45 degrees. The upward movement of the bent-knee sit-up exercise begins by flexing the neck to move the chin nearer to (but not touching) the upper chest and then curling the torso to lift the upper back off the mat. During the upward movement, the lifter should maintain the same stationary lower body position with the arms folded across the chest and not lift the feet off the mat as the upper body is raised. The lifter should continue to curl the torso toward the thighs until the upper back is off the mat.
summary: Instructions for performing a bent-knee sit-up exercise, emphasizing proper body alignment and controlled movement.

content: The starting position for the abdominal crunch is lying in a supine position on a floor mat. Place the heels on a bench with the hips and knees flexed to approximately 90 degrees. The thighs, knees, and feet should be aligned with each other. Fold the arms across the chest or abdomen. All repetitions begin from this position. Begin the abdominal crunch by flexing the neck to move the chin nearer to (but not touching) the upper chest and then curl the torso to lift the upper back off the mat. Maintain the same stationary lower body position with the arms folded across the chest; do not lift the feet off the bench as the upper body is raised. Continue to curl the torso toward the thighs until the upper back is off the mat. Uncurl the torso, then extend the neck slowly and under control back to the starting position; do not lift the buttocks off the floor to help rebound for the next repetition or lift the feet from the bench. Maintain the same stationary lower body position and keep the arms folded across the chest.
summary: Detailed steps for performing an abdominal crunch exercise, highlighting proper form and controlled movement.

content: The feet should be hip-width apart or slightly closer, with the foot, ankle, knee, and hip of each leg in a vertical plane. The palms of the hands should be flat on the floor approximately shoulder-width apart with the elbows pointing backward. Drop the elbows to the floor one at a time and position them directly under the shoulders with the forearms parallel to each other. Move the feet back one at a time to allow the hips and knees to extend so the abdomen and the front of the hips and legs rest on the floor. Elevate the hips so the ankles, knees, hips, shoulders, and head are in a straight line at a slight incline (the head end of the body will be higher than the feet end). Isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position with the hips posteriorly tilted to keep the lumbar spine in a neutral position. Keep the elbows directly under the shoulders. Maintain a neutral head position. At the end of the repetition, slowly lower the hips and legs to return to the starting position.
summary: Instructions for performing a plank exercise, emphasizing proper body alignment and core engagement.

content: Lie on the floor on the left side with the left elbow under the left shoulder. Position the left forearm perpendicular to the torso. Stack the right foot on top of the left foot with the right leg evenly on top of the left leg, or place the right foot on the floor immediately in front of the left foot. Stack the right arm evenly on top of the right side of the torso. The body’s weight will be supported by the left arm. Use the outside of the left foot as the pivot, or anchor point, and elevate the hips so the left ankle, knee, hip, and shoulder are in a straight line. Keep the right foot and leg stacked on top of the left foot and leg—or alternatively, keep the right foot on the floor in front of the left foot—and the right arm stacked on top of the right side of the torso. Isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position. Keep the left elbow directly under the left shoulder. Keep the head in a neutral position with the eyes focused forward. At the end of the repetition, slowly lower the hips and legs to return to the starting position. Repeat the exercise for the right side.
summary: Instructions for performing a single-leg hip raise on the floor.

content: Kneel facing the stability ball with the toes on the floor, upper body in an erect position, elbows extended, and hands touching the upper front side of the ball. While keeping the hands on the ball, reposition the knee and toe location to create a 90-degree angle at the knees and ankles with the knees, hips, and shoulders in a near-vertical plane. Keep the knees and toes on the floor; the elbows fully extended; the arms parallel to each other; and the knees, hips, and shoulders in a straight line. Extend the knees and flex the shoulders to roll the ball forward; keep the arms across the top of the ball until it comes close to the face. Isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position; do not let the hips sag toward the floor. At the end of the repetition, flex the knees and extend the shoulders to roll the arms back over the ball to return to the starting position.
summary: Instructions for performing a stability ball roll-out.

content: Kneel in front of a stability ball in a quadruped position with the body facing away from the ball, the hands underneath the shoulders approximately shoulder-width apart, and the knees underneath the hips approximately hip-width apart. Place the feet, one at a time, next to each other on top of the stability ball. While keeping the feet on the ball, extend the hips and knees and reposition the hands so that the body is in a prone plank position with the hands directly underneath the shoulders. Isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position with the elbows fully extended and the head in a neutral position. Maintain these positions during the exercise. All repetitions begin from this prone plank position. The exercise begins with the knees and elbows fully extended and the torso in a rigid position. The hips are flexed to roll the ball toward the chest until the toes are on top of the ball and the hips are directly over the shoulders. The head is kept in a neutral position during the upward movement. Return to the starting position by allowing the hips to extend under control with the knees and elbows fully extended and the torso held rigid. The head is kept in a neutral position during the downward movement.
summary: Instructions for performing a stability ball roll-out with a prone plank starting position.

content: Begin in a quadruped position with the body facing away from the stability ball, the hands underneath the shoulders approximately shoulder-width apart, and the knees underneath the hips approximately hip-width apart. Place the feet, one at a time, next to each other on top of the stability ball. While keeping the feet on the ball, extend the hips and knees and reposition the hands so that the body is in a prone plank position with the hands directly underneath the shoulders. Isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position with the elbows fully extended and the head in a neutral position. All repetitions begin from this prone plank position. While keeping the knees and elbows fully extended and the torso in a rigid position, begin the exercise by raising the hips slightly and flexing the hips and knees to roll the ball toward the chest until the hips and knees are fully flexed. The shoulders are kept over the hands and the head in a neutral position.
summary: Instructions for performing a stability ball roll-out with a quadruped starting position.

content: The starting position for the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise is to sit in the machine with the feet on the floor and the legs behind the roller pads. The hips and knees should be flexed to approximately 90 degrees with the legs behind and in contact with the roller pads in the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) starting position. The back of the upper arms should be pressed against the arm pads while grasping the handles of the machine on each side of the head in the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) starting position. All repetitions of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise begin from the starting position. The forward movement of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise involves flexing the torso to move the chest forward and down toward the thighs. The hips and lower back should not be lifted from the pad during the forward movement of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise. The torso should be flexed until the elbows point toward the thighs during the forward movement of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise. The backward movement of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise involves extending the torso slowly and under control back to the starting position. The hips should not be lifted or excessively extended during the backward movement of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise. The lower body position should remain stationary with the hands grasping the handles of the machine during the backward movement of the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise.
summary: Instructions for performing the Abdominal Crunch (Machine) exercise, including starting position, movement, and form.

content: The starting position for the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise is to sit on a stability ball with the feet flat on the floor. The hips and knees should be flexed to approximately 90 degrees in the starting position for the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise. The thighs, knees, and feet should be aligned with each other in the starting position for the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise. The feet should be walked away from the ball until the mid to lower section of the back is in contact with the ball and the torso is approximately parallel to the floor in the starting position for the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise. The arms can be folded across the chest or held near the sides of the head in the starting position for the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise. All repetitions of the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise begin from the starting position. Begin the exercise by flexing the neck to move the chin nearer to (but not touching) the upper chest and then curl the torso to lift the upper back off the ball. Maintain the same stationary lower body position and keep the arms folded across the chest or near the sides of the head; do not lift the feet off the floor as the upper body is raised. Continue to curl the torso toward the thighs until the upper back is off the ball. Uncurl the torso, then extend the neck slowly and under control back to the starting position; do not lift or excessively extend the hips to help rebound for the next repetition. Maintain the same stationary lower body position and keep the arms folded across the chest or near the sides of the head.
summary: Instructions for performing the Stability Ball Abdominal Crunch exercise, including starting position, movement, and form.

content: The starting position for the Stability Ball Plank exercise is to kneel in front of and facing a stability ball. Roll forward onto the ball so that the abdomen rests on the top of the ball in a prone plank position, the torso is as parallel to the floor as body proportions allow, and the hands are positioned directly underneath or slightly in front of the shoulders on the floor. Hold the feet together throughout the exercise. Isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position with the elbows fully extended and the head in a neutral position. Maintain these positions during the exercise. All repetitions begin from this prone plank position. Extend at the hips to bring the legs up in a straight line with the body. Keep the lower body in a rigid plank position with the spine held in a neutral position and the ankles in dorsiflexion. Do not rotate the hips during the movement. Return to the starting position by allowing the hips to flex under control until the feet touch the floor. Keep the lower body in a rigid plank position with the spine held in a neutral position and the ankles in dorsiflexion.
summary: Instructions for performing the Stability Ball Plank exercise, including starting position, movement, and form.

content: Stand straddling a kettlebell with the feet flat between hip- and shoulder-width apart and the toes pointed straight ahead. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and the elbows fully extended. The hands should be using a closed, pronated grip.
summary: Starting position for a kettlebell exercise, emphasizing hip and shoulder positioning.

content: Stand straddling a kettlebell with the feet flat between hip- and shoulder-width apart and the toes pointed straight ahead. Position the body with the back in a neutral spine position. The shoulders should be retracted and depressed. The heels should be in contact with the floor. The eyes should be focused ahead. The hands are holding the kettlebell between the thighs with the elbows fully extended. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the kettlebell swing: feet shoulder-width apart, neutral spine, and kettlebell held between the thighs.

content: Begin the exercise by flexing at the hips (with the knees held in the same starting position) and swinging the kettlebell between the legs. Keep the knees in a moderately flexed position with the back neutral and the elbows extended during the backward movement. Keep swinging the kettlebell between the legs until the torso is nearly parallel to the floor and the kettlebell is past the vertical line of the body. When the backward swing reaches its end point, reverse the movement of the kettlebell by extending the hips and knees to move the kettlebell forward in an upward arc. Allow momentum to raise the kettlebell to approximately eye level. Keep the elbows extended and the back in a neutral spine position.
summary: Kettlebell swing movement: hip hinge, swing between legs, reverse movement to lift.

content: Allow the kettlebell to drop into the downswing; when the upper arms come into contact with the torso, flex the hips and knees to absorb the weight of the kettlebell. Keep the elbows fully extended and the back in a neutral position. Continue the downward-then-backward movement of the kettlebell by flexing the hips and knees until the kettlebell passes under and then behind the vertical line of the body. At the completion of the set, slow and then stop the momentum of the kettlebell and set it down on the floor.
summary: Kettlebell swing downswing: absorb weight, continue backward swing, control the descent.

content: The initial position for the single-leg squat exercise is identical to the initial position of the back squat exercise. If a bar is used for the single-leg squat, it is commonly placed above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck in the high bar placement position. If dumbbells are used for the single-leg squat, they should be held at the sides of the torso with a closed, neutral grip. If spotting is needed for the single-leg squat, two spotters should be used with one on each side of the bar.
summary: Single-leg squat setup: similar to back squat, with bar or dumbbells, spotters if needed.

content: Stand in front of a bench or box that is approximately knee-height with the feet shoulder- to hip-width apart for the single-leg squat. Face away from the bench or box and place the instep of the right foot on top of the bench or box for the single-leg squat. Allow both knees to be slightly flexed with the torso in a nearly erect position and the shoulders held back, the head in a neutral position in relation to the spine, and the chest held up and out to create a neutral or slightly arched back for the single-leg squat. All repetitions of the single-leg squat begin from this position.
summary: Single-leg squat starting position: feet shoulder-width apart, right foot on bench, torso upright.

content: Flex the hip and knee of both legs simultaneously to lower the body in a vertical plane while keeping the torso-to-floor angle constant during the downward movement of the single-leg squat; do not round the upper back or lean forward as the bar is lowered. Keep the heel of the left foot flat on the floor and the instep of the right foot on top of the bench or box during the downward movement of the single-leg squat. Continue flexing the hips and knees until the left thigh is approximately parallel to the floor during the downward movement of the single-leg squat.
summary: Single-leg squat descent: controlled lowering, torso angle maintained, left thigh parallel to floor.

content: Raise the bar under control by actively extending the left hip and knee during the upward movement of the single-leg squat; focus on the left hip and knee, although the right hip and knee will passively extend as well. Maintain a position with a neutral spine and the torso upright during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Extend the hips and knees at the same rate to keep the torso-to-floor angle constant during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Do not allow the body’s weight to shift forward onto the ball of the left foot during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Keep the left knee aligned over the left foot; do not allow the knee to shift inward or outward as it extends during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Continue focusing on extending the left hip and knee to return to the starting position during the upward movement of the single-leg squat.
summary: Single-leg squat ascent: controlled extension, neutral spine, torso upright, focus on left hip and knee.

content: Return to the starting position by allowing the hips to flex under control until the feet touch the floor.
summary: End of single-leg squat: controlled return to starting position.

content: Maintain a position with a neutral spine and the torso upright during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Extend the hips and knees at the same rate to keep the torso-to-floor angle constant during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Do not allow the body’s weight to shift forward onto the ball of the left foot during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Keep the left knee aligned over the left foot; do not allow the knee to shift inward or outward as it extends during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. Continue focusing on extending the left hip and knee to return to the starting position during the upward movement of the single-leg squat. After completing a set with the left leg as the forward leg, repeat the procedure with the right leg as the forward leg for the single-leg squat.
summary: Instructions for performing the single-leg squat, emphasizing proper form and alignment.

content: This exercise can be performed with the kettlebell held in the hand of the same side of the body as the support leg (ipsilateral) or in the hand of the opposite side of the body as the support leg (contralateral) for the single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift. The text and the photos depict the contralateral single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift exercise. With the right hand, grasp a kettlebell with a closed, pronated grip for the single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift. Stand on the left leg (as the support leg) with the hips and shoulders over the left foot and the body’s weight on the left heel for the single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift. The torso should be fully erect, with the shoulders held back for the single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift.
summary: Describes variations and setup for the contralateral single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift.

content: The exercise begins with the back straight, the head in a neutral position in relation to the spine, and the chest held up and out. Hold the kettlebell in front of the right thigh with the right elbow fully extended and the right foot slightly staggered back. All repetitions begin from this position. Allow the left support knee to slightly flex, and then rigidly hold that position throughout the movement. Begin the exercise by allowing the torso to flex forward at the hip of the left support leg. Keep the right shoulder, hip, knee, and ankle aligned as the torso flexes forward; the movement takes place only at the hip of the left support leg. Do not rotate the hips as the kettlebell is lowered. Keep the knee of the left support leg in a slightly flexed position with the shoulders held in the same retracted position. Keep the back neutral with the head in a neutral position and the right elbow still fully extended. Lower the kettlebell until the torso and the right leg are approximately parallel to the floor. Extend the hip of the left support leg to return to the starting position. Keep the knee of the left support leg in a slightly flexed position and the back neutral during the upward movement. Do not hyperextend the torso or neck, or flex the right elbow.
summary: Detailed instructions for the downward and upward phases of the single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift.

content: After completing a set with the left leg as the support leg and the kettlebell held in the right hand, repeat the exercise with the right leg as the support leg and the kettlebell held in the left hand.
summary: Instructions for alternating support legs and kettlebell sides in the single-leg kettlebell Romanian deadlift.

content: Begin the exercise by lying on the floor in a supine position with a kettlebell near the left shoulder. Slightly turn toward the kettlebell and grasp it with the left hand in a closed, pronated grip. Use the right hand to help lift the kettlebell. The bell of the kettlebell should be positioned against the back of the hand and wrist. Flex the left hip and knee to approximately a 45-degree angle with the left foot flat on the floor. Position the right leg flat on the floor with the right ankle dorsiflexed to point the toes up. Lift the kettlebell over the face with both arms and then remove the right hand. The grip of the kettlebell in the left hand should remain near the corner of the handle and near the thumb on the palm of the hand. Keep the left elbow fully extended with the left arm pointing toward the ceiling during the exercise to hold the kettlebell over the left shoulder. Position the right arm flat on the floor at approximately a 45-degree angle away from the right side of the body with the elbow fully extended. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position and setup for the single-arm kettlebell row.

content: While keeping the eyes focused on the kettlebell, begin the exercise by pushing up against the kettlebell. To do so, simultaneously push into the floor with the left foot to rotate the hips and torso so that the body is balanced on the right forearm and right hip. Keep the right leg, right ankle, left knee, and left foot in the same starting position. Continue to press up against the kettlebell and transition from the right forearm to the right hand.
summary: Instructions for the upward movement of the single-arm kettlebell row.

content: Stand straddling a kettlebell with feet flat between hip- and shoulder-width apart and the toes pointed straight ahead. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and the right elbow fully extended; grasp the kettlebell handle with the right hand using a closed, pronated grip. Stand up to lift the kettlebell off the floor, then position the body with the back neutral, the shoulders retracted and depressed, the heels in contact with the floor, and the eyes focused ahead. The right hand holds the kettlebell in front of the right thigh with the elbow fully extended. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the kettlebell swing: standing with feet shoulder-width apart, grasping the kettlebell with a closed grip, and standing upright with a neutral spine.

content: Begin the exercise by flexing the torso farther forward at the hips (with the knees held in the same starting position) and swinging the kettlebell between the legs. Keep the knees in a moderately flexed position with the back neutral and the right elbow extended during the backward movement. Keep swinging the kettlebell between the legs until the torso is nearly parallel to the floor and the kettlebell is past the vertical line of the body.
summary: Initiating the swing: flexing forward at the hips, swinging the kettlebell between the legs while maintaining a neutral back and moderately flexed knees.

content: When the backward swing reaches its end point, reverse the movement of the kettlebell by extending the hips and knees to move the kettlebell forward. Rather than allowing momentum to raise the kettlebell in an upward arc to approximately eye level with the elbows extended and the arms moving away from the torso (as in the two-arm kettlebell swing), allow momentum to raise the kettlebell while keeping the right upper arm in contact with the body.
summary: Swinging the kettlebell back up: extending hips and knees to drive the kettlebell forward, keeping the upper arm close to the body.

content: When the kettlebell reaches the position where the right upper arm would move away from the torso, quickly shrug the right shoulder and then flex the right elbow to pull the kettlebell back and up (not in an arc) in front of the torso; this is the transition. As the kettlebell reaches it maximum height, loosen the grip and quickly rotate the right arm under the kettlebell.
summary: Transitioning to the next swing: shrugging the shoulder and flexing the elbow to pull the kettlebell upward, then rotating the arm under the kettlebell at the peak.

content: The lower body will be in a lunge position with the right knee directly underneath the right hip and shoulder, and the left hip and knee at approximately 90-degree angles. Shift the body’s weight to the left leg and push up against the kettlebell; push down toward the floor with the left foot while fully extending the left hip and knee. Complete the upward movement by stepping forward with the right foot to place it next to the left foot with the kettlebell held directly over the left shoulder.
summary: Upward movement in the kettlebell swing: transitioning to a lunge position, pushing up against the kettlebell, and stepping forward to return to a standing position.

content: Begin the downward movement by pressing up against the kettlebell and extending the right leg straight back into a lunge position. Drop the right knee straight down to the floor. The right shoulder, hip, and knee should be in the same vertical plane. While continuing to press up against the kettlebell, focus the eyes on the kettlebell and bring the right hand to the floor.
summary: Downward movement in the kettlebell swing: extending the right leg back into a lunge, lowering the right knee, and bringing the right hand to the floor.

content: The arms will be in a straight line nearly perpendicular to the floor with the kettlebell directly above both shoulders, elbows, and the right hand. Keep the eyes focused on the kettlebell. To do so, simultaneously push into the floor with the left foot to rotate the hips and torso so that the body is balanced on the right forearm and right hip. Keep the right leg, right ankle, left knee, and left foot in the same starting position.
summary: Maintaining balance and posture during the kettlebell swing: keeping the arms straight, eyes focused on the kettlebell, and rotating the hips and torso to maintain balance.

content: With the left foot still flat on the floor, extend the left hip until the left knee is in approximately a 90-degree flexed position. The three points of contact on the floor should be both feet and the right hand. Continue to press up against the kettlebell and transition from the right forearm to the right hand.
summary: Transitioning to the next phase of the swing: extending the left hip, maintaining three points of contact with the floor, and transitioning from the forearm to the hand.

content: After completing a set with the kettlebell in the left hand, repeat the exercise with the kettlebell in the right hand and the right foot flat on the floor.
summary: Switching sides: repeating the exercise with the kettlebell in the opposite hand.

content: Slowly lower the body to the starting position.  Keep the eyes focused on the kettlebell. The left knee should be flexed to approximately 45 degrees, with the right elbow fully extended and the right hand on the floor. Continue to press up against the kettlebell with the left arm fully extended, and lower the right elbow to the floor to support the body’s weight.
summary: Returning to the starting position: slowly lowering the body, maintaining focus on the kettlebell, and supporting the body's weight.

content: The right shoulder, hip, and knee should be in the same vertical plane. Be sure the left hip is fully extended and aligned with the shoulder and knee and that the right knee is extended and the ankle is dorsiflexed. Flex the left hip to drop the body down to sit on the floor.
summary: Lowering into the starting position: aligning the right shoulder, hip, and knee, ensuring proper hip and knee alignment, and flexing the hip to lower the body.

content: While keeping the eyes focused on the kettlebell, begin the exercise by pushing up against the kettlebell.  Keep the eyes focused on the kettlebell.
summary: Starting the exercise: pushing up against the kettlebell while maintaining focus on it.

content: Stand straddling a kettlebell with the feet flat and hip- to shoulder-width apart and the toes pointed straight ahead. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and the elbows fully extended; grasp the kettlebell on both sides of the handle with a closed, neutral grip. Return to a standing position with the kettlebell held near the chest, the back in a neutral spine position, the shoulders retracted and depressed, the heels in contact with the floor, and the eyes focused ahead. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the kettlebell squat: feet shoulder-width apart, squat down to grasp the kettlebell, return to standing with a neutral spine and engaged core.

content: Begin the downward movement by flexing the hips and knees slowly and under control. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and keep the kettlebell close to the body; do not allow the heels to rise off the floor during the downward movement. Keep the knees aligned over the feet; do not allow the knees to shift inward or outward as they flex. Continue the downward movement until one of these three events occurs (they determine the maximum range of motion, or the lowest squat position): 1. The thighs are parallel to the floor (if achievable). 2. The torso begins to round or flex forward. 3. The heels rise off the floor. Actual squat depth is dependent on lower body joint flexibility. Keep the body tight and under control; do not bounce or relax the legs or torso at the bottom of the movement.
summary: Kettlebell squat descent: controlled hip and knee flexion, maintain neutral spine, keep kettlebell close, avoid heel lift, stop at parallel, torso rounding, or heel lift.

content: Stand up under control by extending the hips and knees. Maintain a neutral or slightly arched back and keep the kettlebell close to the body. Resist the tendency to lean forward by keeping the head tilted slightly back and holding the chest up and out. Continue pushing up with the body’s weight evenly distributed between the heel and midfoot of both feet to keep them in contact with the floor and the hips under the body’s center of mass. Do not allow the body’s weight to shift forward onto the balls of the feet. Keep the knees aligned over the feet; do not allow the knees to shift inward or outward as they extend. Continue the upward movement at an even rate until the hips and knees are fully extended to return to the starting position.
summary: Kettlebell squat ascent: controlled hip and knee extension, maintain neutral spine, resist leaning forward, distribute weight evenly, keep knees aligned.

content: Lie supine on the floor with a stability ball near the feet. Place the heels and calves on the upper front side of the ball with the ankles dorsiflexed. The knees should be fully extended with the head, back, and buttocks flat on the floor. Position the arms on the floor perpendicular to the torso with the elbows fully extended. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the stability ball exercise: lie supine with heels on the ball, knees extended, arms perpendicular to the body.

content: To begin the one-arm kettlebell press, unrack the kettlebell from the right shoulder, uncurl the right arm at the elbow, lower the kettlebell to arm’s length between the thighs, and allow the momentum of the kettlebell to drop it into the downswing between the legs. Flex the hips and knees to absorb the weight of the kettlebell and then follow the guidelines for the backward movement. After completing a set with the right hand holding the kettlebell, repeat the exercise with the left hand holding the kettlebell.
summary: One-arm kettlebell press setup: unrack kettlebell, lower to thighs, initiate backward swing, repeat with opposite hand.

content: The starting position, backward movement, and forward/upward movement guidelines of the one-arm kettlebell clean exercise apply to the one-arm kettlebell press exercise. All repetitions of the one-arm kettlebell press begin from the starting position. Begin the one-arm kettlebell press exercise by flexing the left shoulder and extending the left elbow to press the kettlebell overhead. The right arm remains at the right side of the body during the one-arm kettlebell press exercise.
summary: One-arm kettlebell press: follows the same movement pattern as the one-arm kettlebell clean, with the right arm remaining stationary.

content: As the kettlebell reaches the position where the right upper arm would move away from the torso, quickly shrug the right shoulder and then flex the right elbow to pull the kettlebell back and up (not in an arc) in front of the torso; this is the transition. When the kettlebell reaches it maximum height, loosen the grip and quickly rotate the right arm under the kettlebell. Catch the kettlebell in a racked position with the right upper arm next to the torso; the right elbow pointing toward the floor; and the kettlebell against the back of the right hand, wrist, or forearm (depending on the size of the kettlebell and length of the arm).
summary: One-arm kettlebell press transition: shrug and flex right elbow to pull kettlebell upward, rotate arm under kettlebell, catch in racked position.

content: Keep the spine neutral and the eyes looking forward during the upward movement of the one-arm kettlebell press exercise. Do not allow the spine to flex laterally during the upward movement of the one-arm kettlebell press exercise. Allow the kettlebell to lower slowly and under control back to the starting position during the downward movement of the one-arm kettlebell press exercise. Do not flex the torso forward as the kettlebell is lowered during the downward movement of the one-arm kettlebell press exercise. As the kettlebell is lowered during the downward movement of the one-arm kettlebell press exercise, internally rotate the left shoulder so that the left wrist and forearm move back to the starting, neutral position.
summary: One-arm kettlebell press form: maintain neutral spine, control movement, avoid lateral flexion, internally rotate shoulder on descent.

content: Keep swinging the kettlebell between the legs until the torso is nearly parallel to the floor and the kettlebell is past the vertical line of the body. When the backward swing reaches its end point, reverse the movement of the kettlebell by extending the hips and knees to move the kettlebell forward. Rather than allowing momentum to raise the kettlebell in an upward arc to approximately eye level with the elbows extended and the arms moving away from the torso (as in the two-arm kettlebell swing), allow momentum to raise the kettlebell while keeping the right upper arm in contact with the body.
summary: One-arm kettlebell swing setup: swing kettlebell between legs, reverse movement, keep right arm in contact with body.

content: Lie supine on the floor with a stability ball near the feet. Place the heels and calves on the upper front side of the ball with the ankles dorsiflexed. The knees should be fully extended with the head, back, and buttocks flat on the floor. Position the arms on the floor perpendicular to the torso with the elbows fully extended. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the stability ball bridge exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by extending the hips so that the body is in a straight line from the shoulders to the ankles (Bridge). Keep the hips rigid throughout the movement; do not let them sag toward the floor. Keep the head and arms in the same starting position and the legs parallel to each other. Continue bringing the heels toward the buttocks until the knees are flexed to approximately 90 degrees. Return to the starting position by extending the knees under control. Keep the head neutral and in contact with the floor.
summary: Instructions for performing the bridge portion of the stability ball exercise.

content: Flex the knees to roll the ball toward the body and bring the heels in toward the buttocks (Curl).
summary: Instructions for performing the curl portion of the stability ball exercise.

content: Assume the top push-up position with the elbows fully extended; hold a dumbbell in each hand approximately shoulder-width apart using a closed, neutral grip. Keep the head, neck, shoulders, spine, hips, thighs, knees, lower legs, and ankles aligned. Keep the feet hip- to shoulder-width apart to maintain balance throughout the movement. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Starting position for the dumbbell row exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by allowing the shoulders to extend and the elbows to flex to lower the body toward the floor. Maintain a neutral head position and isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position. Continue lowering the body until it is close to the floor. Flex the shoulders and extend the elbows to push the body back up to the starting position. Maintain a neutral head position and isometrically hold the torso in a rigid position.
summary: Instructions for performing the lowering and pushing phases of the dumbbell row exercise.

content: After reaching the starting position, isometrically contract the core muscles and the muscles of the left shoulder, then retract the right scapula, extend the right shoulder, and flex the right elbow to pull the right dumbbell up alongside the right side of the body. After the dumbbell is alongside the body, pause and then lower the dumbbell back to the floor. Maintain the head, torso, and lower body positions, and repeat the rowing motion with the left arm (not seen in the photos); this sequence is one repetition.
summary: Instructions for performing the rowing motion with each arm during the dumbbell row exercise.

content: The National Strength and Conditioning Association (NSCA) is the world’s leading organization in the field of sport conditioning. The NSCA draws on the resources and expertise of the most recognized professionals in strength and conditioning, sport science, performance research, education, and sports medicine. The NSCA is the world’s trusted source of knowledge and training guidelines for coaches and athletes.
summary: Description of the National Strength and Conditioning Association (NSCA).
